author: Cryptoshi
content: <@717554586843611217> I heard you mentioned on trading floor that we should not be taking any trades on Tesla today as there were no setups. I followed your 5 MIN H/L retest strategy on Tesla today and this was the outcome. Now I understand that just cuz I had a winning trade doesn't mean it was the right thing to do. I want to understand in more detail why you advised against trading tesla today even though it met criteria for one of the trading strategies you have covered on youtube. I think understanding this will help me bridge the gap on when not to trade which I think is more important then knowing when to trade. Please note my first trade was a loser due to tight SL to maintain 1:2 RR. Second trade gave me a better entry with 1:2 RR. I exited with 1:2 RR and didn't wait for price to reach 163.77 key level.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: Can u explain you strategy please? Where would your SL be and where would you take profit? Also if you're trading B&R how would you qualify your first trade? And do you normally trade on 2 min or 1 min?

author: 👑 of Puts
content: It's just my thought but this is range trade not B&R.

author: Cryptoshi
content: Correct it is not B/R. It is a retest. Tony covered in one of his videos. he expects 2 things that can happen at this level. Retest or Break and Retest. I saw this retested.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: If range trading is part of your strategy then it's ok imho

author: PlugSabe
content: Yeah so I only trade the B/R. I always wait for a signal candle and my SL will go slightly above the high of the candle. On the first trade I just targeted a 2:1 R and the second trade I targeted LOD which was the same level. I consider the first trader to be a B/R because it closed and held below the PML then failed to close back above on that big wick. I watch the 1min for an entry first, but if the candle closes above the level, then I watch to see if the 2min confirms which is wat happened in this case. Iv backtested the 1 and 2 min and for my strategy they have the same winrate, I just take whichever confirms.

author: JLD
content: <@717554586843611217>  <@801169196015943731> I took a trade today on AAPL and lost. I saw that price was slowly working its way higher creating higher lows and then holding the $174.94 line and I decided to enter inon a call.  I ended up getting stopped out quickly. If I would have placed my stop loss lower the trade would have worked out in my favor. My question is was I right to get in on this trade based on what I saw and just made a mistake with my stop position? Or was this not a good set up? I think I see where I should have placed my stop, but am not positive. I only lost 22 dollars on this trade. Playing small while I learn:) Thanks for the input.

author: Jdub
content: The answer is both. For apple wise since its consoldating you have to be able to give it a little bit more room. When the markets are in a range like that its hard to have precision entries and stops. Trading is a lot easier when its trending in one direction. So in this case you either have a tighter stop and look for a re entry or you have a wider stop and let the trade breath a little bit and have more risk.

author: Jdub
content: 172 was the pdl for aapl. That is the key level. Once price opened before that was the level to look out for. It ended up getting above that level for a little bit but as you look back on the charts and compare aapl to the qqq you will see the relative weakenss it had and it was also underneath the 172 level again. As for the key levels you have to look left on the higher time frames.

author: Jdub
content: An order block valid as long as it doesn't close below it. But remeber that there is a difference between high probability order blocks vs low probabilities. The ones you want to look for entries is when there is multiple confluences.

content: The real retest was off of Fridays high on June 14th. You also have to remember that everyone is the morning was targeting PDH as a target so when price moved there everyone was taking profits. author: crunchybrewski
content: Not entirely sure what you mean by pair. Typically in this room at least, Tony and JDub preach to compare the individual ticker to QQQ or SPY which represent general market sentiment. So you want to look for long positions when qqq is strong, and short when qqq is weak. There are cases where there are high timeframe objectives where that is ignored. But generally that is the case. Another critical part of the break and retest strategy is "large displacement" from the range. It looks to me like NFLX and ZOOM had some amount of displacement that seems reasonable to try a trade. One of those would have worked while the other wouldnt. 50% is still a profitable outcome with a 2R trade.

author: crunchybrewski
content: Oh, I forgot one thing. It's not only *that* it retests, but *how* it retests. You want the candles to retest with strong price action rejecting your level to confirm your thesis. I'll leave it up to you to watch the modules on exactly what that means though. Goodluck

author: Jdub
content: When your backtesting you backtest every single day. You gather all the data and then you start to be more specific. If the strategy or setup doesn't have a positive expectancy over a general period there is not point in being specific. Once the setup has a possible edge thats where you go back and add mutiple confluences and other thiings into your backtesting

author: Papa
content: Tony's entry was on the first hammer candle that touched above the OB, I believe entry was right after/near the close of that candle.  You will be able to hear it in the trade review or live trade recording that Jack posts.  The second entry/add would be where Jack called it out and Tony did mention prior he would be interested in adding if there's a candle that wicked toward the bottom of the OB, which is two candles over to the left of your arrow.  I also added simultaneously when Jack made that call because that is the retest system/playbook.  The first chart you had I believe there was a key swing high from a few days/week ago around 191-ish, which is where I initially entered on the retest.   This also coincided with the opening range break on the 5 minute candle (multiple confluences).  I closed at HOD and then re-entered based on the 1 MIN OB on the current drawing.

author: 👑 of Puts
content: you took short where i went long. My setup is retest though. Opening print is the first 1 min candle so you don't seem to have it marked correctly. I've done backtesting on this setup and in long run it works great. If it's above 1 min opening range i look for call. Below it, i look for puts. As you can see later in the day it reversed and there was a better entry with clear PT.  If you have a reversal setup you need to have clear understanding of it and play it at least from key levels (in the long run reversal never worked for me so i don't play it) But that 481.64 level i had from Wednesday low would have been a good shot at entry for reversal.

Hi <@693264634953596948> 
Trying to answer your question: Why not enter at the 9:56 hammer candle?

In this image you see the OB in blue, but it would be complete drawn if the blue box to 115.20ish, so the PA actually went deep in the OB but never invalidated the trade, it went down again and with some momentum when it broke the OB to the down and then retested I took my chances and went well. Look how impulsive was the candle at 10:04, that was the PA that was looking for

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
One of the aspects I appreciate most about Break and Retest and Momentum Trading is the flexibility it offers. 
Even if you find yourself entering a position a bit early or late, the prevailing momentum and trend can often still provide a favorable push in the right direction. 
This buffer can be a game-changer, allowing for more adaptability and reducing the pressure of pinpoint timing.

author: crunchybrewski
content: In my opinion, there was no trade on GOOG until way late in the day.  My system (very strict) used explicit key levels and uses OBs and FVGs just above or below as confirmation. No levels were retested until something like 2:30 P, when most of the volume has left the market. Technically you can use the double top at 12:45 bc it was testing an OB and showed weakness, but that's not in my system.  The QQQ trade had a beautiful entry a smidge after 10 (where I find the most success). It retested the 5min OR low with confirmation. The peak at 10:30 was nothing but a gap fill to continue lower, so it wouldnt have stopped my system out. As for me, I only saw one entry.

author: Jdub
content: Looks super solid on the QQQ. I personally don't trade later in the day unless its a trend day and it looks like this was a nice trend day towards the downside on the QQQ with the 5 Min OB

author: Cryptoshi
content: I will try to take a stab it. Tesla had overall strength to the upside going into open yesterday. However, the Qs were heavy and we saw a lot of downward pressure on the Qs. Due to this we were looking to enter shorts on A+ setups. Now this is not an A+ setup because you can see that Tesla was above its PDH. So shorting near the PDH even when it meets our B/R criteria for ORB - 5min doesn't meet the overall HTF context. So I believe even though price action and B/R strategy played out well here, the HTF context was not supportive of this trade.

content:
Hi <@801169196015943731> , <@717554586843611217>  <@1014389514141184074> ,

Could you please share your thoughts on my trade? Today, I was watching TSLA after it pulled back. QQQ was weak, and TSLA wasn’t particularly strong either, showing downside movement (as you can see my TP1 and TP2). My stop loss was set above the 1-minute candle. Initially, I saw a good retest from the 1-minute candle, and I entered the trade when I saw strong price action to the downside. At the time, it was within the 1-minute order block, and the 5-minute chart also looked good for a downside move, with QQQ still showing weakness.

However, TSLA retested the 5-minute candle's open range (OR) -221.12  and pulled back. Once the candle closed above the 1-minute OR, I got stopped out. I entered again with the same thesis, but I got stopped out again.

Could you please let me know if my stop loss was too tight? After that, I had to head to work, and honestly, I was a bit frustrated because after I left, the stock moved in the direction I was expecting. LOL

author: maitys
content: Did some backtesting on a scalping strategy this weekend with these results (69 trades over 11 days). Not sure if this strategy is worth trying out in real though with the given results, mainly because of the low Avg win/loss trade ratio. Would be great if anyone has some feedback. Thanks so much! (I made sure to backtest on Jan 2024 days since I dont remember the daily trends from back then)

author: crunchybrewski
content: It all depends. Your RR isnt the greatest, but your winrate is high. The only issue with more trades is more fees. But you have a profitable setup over those 11 days.  I use expected value to decide if I want to compare systems. In your case the expected value of the trade EV = .72 * 39 - (1-.72)*32. So your expected value is like 37$/trade.

author: maitys
content: got it. thanks for the feedback! this is very helpful. Yes, my main focus was to keep a decent win rate so I was keeping my RR small. I will continue testing this over another 11 days where I keep an RR of atleast 2 and see what comes out. Surely Win rate will go down but hopefully will result in better EV. (Also, it will reduce the number of trades I take which is good)

author: Papa
content: Sorry that this trade didn't work out for you.  From what I can see price did not confirm continuation to the downside, ie need to make sure it closes below PDL for a B/R.  That did not occur at the time of your trade entry, the candle you highlighted does not have good price action for the direction of your trade.  META in premarket was inside the prior day so you don't really want to take a trade until it breaks the PDL with displacement on a B/R if your trade thesis was to the downside.  Also keep in mind that the premarket levels are liquidity zones as well, so they usually need to be tested/swept.  Hope this makes sense.

author: wherz
content: I have a bad habit of exiting out of my trades early ( I chicken out even when I plan a trade) how do you fix that? <@1121621751764369501>

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
BOS (Break of Structure): This refers to a price movement that breaks through a previously established price level. For example, when the price is in a dominant trend (either an uptrend or downtrend), it typically respects the trend and breaks into a new structure by surpassing a "Higher High (HH)" or falling below a "Lower Low (LL)."

Buying Contracts: I typically buy contracts at the 10th candle. (TradingView often messes up my circle annotations, haha.) The 10th candle often shows seller pressure driving the price down. Everyone has a different trading style, so you can focus on the general retest area or the exact breakout candle.

Selling Decision: I would sell based on the price touching yesterday’s daily low.

author: Gaby
content: What do you look at the volume? Thank you

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Gabiruchis - When I look at volume, here’s what I focus on:

Trends: Increasing volume during a price move confirms the trend's strength, while declining volume can hint at a reversal or weakening.
Spikes: Sudden large volume spikes often signal key events like breakouts or reversals. I check how the price reacts afterward.
Key Levels: Higher volume at support or resistance levels shows those levels are significant and worth paying attention to.
Divergence: If the price is making new highs or lows but volume is dropping, it could signal a possible reversal.
Breakouts: Strong breakouts usually happen with high volume, confirming the move is real.

author: RocketRacer
content:
Hello All,
I am not sure if this is common knowledge but I was hoping to get some confirmation from those in the know. It appears that the farther out of the money you take a contract the larger the % loss you will experience on a drawdown of the underlying stock. For example, I analyzed both of the TSLA trades that Jack and Tony took today and I am not saying I had exactly the same entries and exits but if you analyze the contracts keeping the entry and exit the same I see the following:
For the first trade if you took:
$360 contract = 26% loss
$365 contract = 29% loss
$370 contract = 32% loss
$375 contract = 34% loss
$380 contract = 37% loss
$385 contract = 39% loss
$390 contract = 41% loss
$395 contract = 43% loss
$400 contract = 47% loss
$405 contract = 50% loss

author: RocketRacer
content:
I also analyzed the 2nd TSLA trade and saw a similar trend:
$360 contract = 18% loss
$365 contract = 19% loss
$370 contract = 20% loss
$375 contract = 23% loss
$380 contract = 25% loss
$385 contract = 27% loss
$390 contract = 30% loss
$395 contract = 32% loss

author: RocketRacer
content: I had been buying cheaper farther out of the money contracts out of necessity because of the expense and also because it would allow me to practice scaling, but it appears that when I have to take a loss the % loss is much higher

author: Yaanii
content: Because of option Greeks but I can’t remember which ones. Buying far out the money is cheaper but if price doesn’t move in your favour the likelihood of those options becoming in the money is small making them loose value faster as they are becoming further out of the money. I stick to 1-2 near the money, if it’s expensive I don’t trade them.

author: RiskManager
content: <@1121621751764369501> Excellent work spotting reversal on AAPL yesterday! Would you share what yo observed in the candles to confirm a reversal and how you determined stop loss? 🙏

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Good Morning Sam - 

To answer your question :  Reversal Confirmation:

I observed a strong bullish rejection around the bottom of a clear demand zone where the price had a previous imbalance. The downward trendline broke as a reversal candle formed with a long lower wick, signaling buyers stepping in.
Following the rejection, a small bullish engulfing candle further confirmed momentum shifting to the upside.
I entered the trade where I labeled "Bought Apple Contracts", as the candles began printing higher lows and the downtrend lost steam.

Stop-Loss Placement:

I placed my stop-loss below the demand zone and the wick of the rejection candle. This aligned with key support and offered a good risk-to-reward ratio while protecting against further downside if the reversal failed.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Hi Gabiruchis,

Thanks for sharing your plan and chart. Looking at QQQ today, I can see why it was tricky—price action looked quite choppy and didn't present a clear A+ setup for clean execution. Here are a few observations to help:

Choppy Structure:

From the chart, QQQ showed a lot of back-and-forth movement, struggling to establish a clear directional bias. This type of market often traps traders on both sides.
Execution Against Levels:

While you planned around the 5-minute levels and targeted the premarket high (536.94), price action failed to show a strong momentum push toward that level after the initial breakout.
Instead, the breakout attempt near the 537 area quickly reversed and formed a Market Structure Shift (MSS) on the 1-minute chart, signaling bearish sentiment.
Buy and Sell Traps:

The "Buy" area was initially a valid trade near trendline support, but price could not sustain higher highs.
The "Sell" reversal shows how critical levels above 537 acted as resistance, and sellers stepped in aggressively.
Lack of A+ Setup:

Overall, today's structure did not offer a clear, high-probability setup.
When price is consolidating or choppy, it’s often better to wait for a clean Break of Structure (BOS), retest confirmation, or stronger momentum.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: MSS (Market Structure Shift) is similar to CHOCH (Change of Character). It serves as an early warning that a potential shift in market structure may occur. This is something to monitor closely, as it depends on pullbacks and the formation of Higher Highs (HH) or Lower Lows (LL) as key confirmations.

author: HighVibeLife
content: <@1121621751764369501> Love your recaps/charts. Thank you! Can you speak to why the stop loss on NVDA when it broke the trend line the second time, and not the first time? Would love to understand how you look at holding through vs. sell all in a situation like that. thanks!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Thanks for the kind words! Let me break this down for you:

Why the stop loss on the second break, not the first:
First Candle's Wick Touches the Trendline:

The first candle's wick tested the trendline but did not decisively break it. This can often be interpreted as a false breakdown or a trendline retest.
The wick bouncing off the trendline can indicate indecision, and the trendline still holds as valid support.
Second Candle's Clear Break and Close Below:

The second candle closed below the trendline with no part of the body touching the trendline or 9 EMA. This signifies that the trendline has been broken decisively.
A candle closing below both the trendline and the 9 EMA is a strong bearish signal, warranting an exit to safeguard capital.

Why Hold Through the First Test:
Holding through the first test can be strategic because trendlines often get retested multiple times. Selling too early might lead to missing a potential bounce or continuation in your favor.
The key is to wait for confirmation of the break, which comes when a candle closes below the trendline (with no touch or retest) and undercuts additional support like the 9 EMA.

When to Sell All Contracts:
The trendline break combined with the close below the 9 EMA signals a shift in momentum.
The decision to sell all contracts was based on the lack of support from both the trendline and the EMA, indicating that the bullish thesis no longer held.

This is a rule of thumb **__for me__**:
When using the tops of ranges, usually I'm aiming to higher continuation
When using the bottoms of ranges, like this 5min ORB, usually I'm aiming to lower continuation

You still need to look at Price Action, but use this as a simple guide

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Let me try to put it simpler:
If you are looking for bullish continuation, then you usually are looking for reactions on the Previous Day High, PreMarket High, High of the Day, etc
If you are looking for bearish continuation, then you usually are looking for reactions on the Previous Day Low, PreMarket Low, Low of the Day, etc

But if your ticker fails to go higher than yHOD, then you could use it as BnR level for going short, however I'd wait until it takes yLOD for having a better quality setup, because then you will trading internal liquidity (scalping) instead of waiting for external liquidity and a bigger move

And you need to be careful as the 1min/5min could look like a failed attempt but the 15min/1hr could be telling a different story (Price is fractal)

This is exactly what Jack said in the Playbook and A+ setups video about aligning all the timeframes for your move. Or said differently, you don't short in the smaller timeframe a ticker than has an uptrend in the HTF

author: demchy19
content:
Hi <@1121621751764369501> or anyone else,
I have a question. On Tuesday, you had a very nice trade on NVDA. I’m curious—would you have entered NVDA if it had filled the gap, broken the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then gone up (green arrow)?
Or, alternatively, would you have entered if it had broken the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, gone up, and then started selling (red arrow)?
I noticed in your trade plan that you entered around 8:19 (orange arrow). and also why yes or no? and where would you have SL?

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: Hey - I will get back to you in an hour or two on this one!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1025807331327475863> 

Great question! Comparing your second chart and my first one, here’s how I’d approach the scenarios you outlined:

Scenario 1 (Green Arrow):
If NVDA filled the gap, broke the 5-minute opening range, retested the bottom line of the opening range, and then pushed up:

Yes, I would consider entering after the retest and confirmation of strength (e.g., a bullish candle closing above the retest level).
This setup aligns with gap-fill momentum, which often leads to a continuation if volume supports it.
Stop Loss (SL): Just below the retest level or slightly under the gap-fill zone to account for noise.
Scenario 2 (Red Arrow):
If NVDA broke the top of the 5-minute opening range, retested, and then started selling:

No, I wouldn’t enter here. The initial breakdown of the top of the range would signal weakness, especially if it couldn’t hold the breakout level.
Entering short after the breakdown could work for a different strategy, but for me, I’d wait for further confirmation that the sellers are in control before considering a trade in this direction.
Entry at 8:19 (Orange Arrow):

I entered because NVDA was forming a bullish triangle (as shown in my chart), signaling consolidation with the likelihood of a breakout. The price was respecting key levels like the premarket high and hourly resistance.
The breakout was accompanied by increasing volume, confirming strength.
SL for this trade: Below the last low of the triangle (around $138.51 on my chart), allowing the price room to breathe while managing risk effectively.
The primary difference is context—whether NVDA respects its key levels (gap-fill, opening range, premarket high) and how volume supports the move. Let me know if that helps clarify!

author: Royal191
content: Yea I trade the New York session. You can technically take a break and retest entry off any key level but have to learn how to identify which level is more relevant so you can take a higher probability trade. For examples levels within the range is not as important because we are looking for momentum trades with continuation. So if price is opening up close to or above PDH we would look for a retest entry around that area for a move to the upside targeting external liquidity.

author: demchy19
content:
Can someone provide me with feedback on this trade on GOOG?
The thesis was that the stock broke the premarket PDL, and once the market opened, the plan was to see if GOOG wanted to come back and retest PDL before entering. However, at the same time, QQQ was showing consolidation, and GOOG was at PDL. So, I entered short, targeting the 5-minute OB/PML.

author: StevenP
content: I was watching this ticker. If you look right before your short order it consolidated first. Broke out and made a 1 min order block. Then retest that order block and moved up making another order block on the 1 min after breaking PDL and retested the OB and didn’t have a candle closure below but showed more strength to the upside invalidating that short in that period of time.

author: StevenP
content: My PDL is in grey below the white line between the two OB’s

author: demchy19
content: First, thank you very much for your feedback. 😄 Nice observation—you caught it right. good job hehe  But if the stock wanted to move lower, what would be your confirmation for entry?

author: StevenP
content: Actually the reason I quit thinking lower was because of the opening drive. I’ll insert another pic

author: StevenP
content: The deep red was the fall off the cliff spot for me at 182.88. It went that way then found buyers. It broke above with a strong green candle and I almost, almost, took a trade long when it had an immediate retest. The very  1st candle on the 1 min made the order block and the 6th green candle respected it.

author: StevenP
content: Now that I look at it after that 6th candle respected the OB and broke the 5 minute OR and had an immediate retest that should’ve been the confirmation to go long.

author: StevenP
content: Sorry I really didn’t answer your question. I wanted to see something along the lines of this…since it didn’t I wasn’t fully invested in the ticker anymore and moved on. What I learned was to watch for the second part of my thesis.

content: No worries, you answered it. I like your process of thinking and how you adapt to the market conditions when the market opens. I need to practice that. For me, I had a thesis, and Jack also mentioned that GOOG looked weak. I was kind of fixated on that, and once GOOG tapped my key level, instead of being patient and waiting for price action, I just jumped in.

author: demchy19
content: Also, based on the CPI, I was more bearish-oriented, which is not okay.

content: In these market conditions, where there isn't a clear direction, it’s really challenging for me. On the higher time frames, it's bullish, but on the lower time frames, it's bearish with quick reversals.

author: DWC2016
content:
🤬😤frustrating trading cycle that's REALLY affecting my mental state...

The cycle : 

1. I enter a trade that initially moves in my favor

2. I take partial profits (a small win) when the trade hits HOD / LOD or some target in between my full target and starts to reverse against me.

3.After I'm stopped out on remaining cons, it returns to my original direction and hits my initial full target and beyond, showing I was right all along.  profit I missed is 5x-10x times larger than what I made.

This pattern happens often and is breaking me mentally.

After this I usually choose overtrade rather than accept weak small wins, even though I know its wrong.

I attached a real example of the small W play I had on the Q's today , 

Should I be scaling more at HOD/LOD ? so I secure more profits and dont feel like this ?  

what do you guys think need help coming up with a better trading plan that feels better mentally so I can stick to my plan.

content: <@802754829004701697> In my opinion, anything taken above PDL was going to be a scalp to PDL/low of the range area, and we needed a break of PDL to truly get out of the range and get to more HTF targets. author: 305 Trader
content: I have been deepening in my understanding this morning on Liquidity and Liquidity sweep. Basically  a strong impulse in the market, is a counter reaction move to a pullback or an "inducement" of the buyers/sellers by the soon-to-become the predominant opposite force to that inducement. This is why I believe the B+R tactic (when patiently applied), is so effective.

author: Angel (az)
content: As the others pointed out, the 5m was incorrectly marked so the retest was different, but I think I see what you were looking at, there was an immediate retest. It's to the downside, we seem to be breaking outside of consolidation after many days and below PDL right into the open with immediate retest. I would say this is quite an aggressive play, because it happens a few minutes into market open. The other comments I have (I make this same mistake myself) - you're taking it very close to LOD, so it is riskier. Ultimately, it did give you a new LOD at 9:42 where you should've taken some off. Thesis checks out for me, but the other factor was QQQ was slowly moving up, and I believe Jack and Tony said in the live that it would be hard to short. I think this is why it hit your targets ultimately, but it was very choppy on 1 minute. 15 minute retest of PML looks nice in hindsight.

author: Angel (az)
content: RR won't work out if you set your stop loss wider, I think it's always better to just re-enter on 84%. Also, we do have a lot more choppier PA recently, so it's better to be more cautious. I also see that you're entering a short off the top of 5 minute OR in the first image? You want to wait for it to get under the low of the OR for the short side. Second image I don't see what level you have B+R on, it doesn't look like a retest to me.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@1336718908257730621> 
🔹 Liquidity Grab & Stop Hunt:

Price dipped below support to trigger stop losses and fill buy orders before reversing.
My chart shows rejection near yesterday’s low (150.231)—a key demand zone.
🔹 Higher Timeframe Structure:

Check 1H/4H charts—this might have been a wick sweep rather than a real breakdown.
Pre-market low (149.941) suggests overall bullish sentiment.
🔹 Order Flow & Demand Zone Rejection:

No strong momentum after the support break = potential fakeout.
Buyers stepped in fast, confirming demand absorption.
🔹 Market Context – USD Strength & News:

Was there news or USD strength influencing the reversal?
How to Improve for Next Time?
✅ Always confirm higher timeframe trend before assuming continuation.
✅ Watch for liquidity zones to avoid getting trapped.
✅ Wait for strong candle close below support, not just wicks.
✅ Look for volume shift & rejection wicks before committing to a trade.

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
I'm also interested in this question. In your case, it's still a decent entry because there's still a good distance to the low of the day. But sometimes, due to a large test candle, there's very little left to the low of the day, with a profit potential of around R1.6.

In such cases, I used to set my first profit target at LOD and the second at R2, but most of the time, the chart wouldn't go beyond LOD. So I'm not sure whether to take such entries when scalping.

On one hand, a strong candle could push further than the LOD, but in practice, that rarely happens for me.

author: DWC2016
content:
yea makes sense , on shorts Im generally more ok with being more aggressive on the entry cause it moves fast but I never really thought to move targets. 

Usually my trade R/R is like 3:1 or 4:1 but I take scale and have trailers along the way and scale / ride them into key levels or what makes sense. in reality its around 2.3 or something. Its just based off the levels on the charts. 

I dont like having big stops is the problem for me usually I like my stops to be about 0.10-0.12% which is about 1/2 a point on the Q's. 

When my stops start being more than that it leads to big losses for me so in this cases if I entered all the way on the second one. In this case my stop would be like 2x of what I normal like them to be. So its kinda tough I guess

And in general on options stops usually lose more than you expect so im super careful about them being small/tight.

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Well, in Google Sheets, I calculate my stop-loss as 20% of the contract price. Visually, it usually ends up being smaller, but that's just to make sure I don’t lose more in a day than I planned. But this is for regular stocks, not indexes, because I didn’t quite understand your 0.10-0.12%, I’m still a beginner.

I’ve also been thinking about reducing my stop-loss because it can be a bit painful to lose money even on stop-losses. But on the other hand, if it's smaller, you'll be stopped out more often, which means more entries following the 84% rule.

Would this be cheaper overall? Maybe, but in my opinion, it would be psychologically harder to keep re-entering trades. But these are just thoughts—I need to test everything in practice.

author: Angel (az)
content: More entries on 84% due to having the wrong stop has definitely been more costly for me, because then its not a true 84%, I just stopped out early. So I do think it's better to follow strictly and stick to the correct stop.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Trade Recap – QQQ (March 6, 2025)
Morning Chop: Multiple BNRs failed, no clean setups, stayed out.
Entry: Short after break & retest of key level.
Trend Confirmation: Strong rejection, followed the downtrend.
Trade Management: Scaled out at TP1 & TP2, respected trendline.
Exit: Closed all remaining contracts when price broke the PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Key Takeaways:

✅ Avoided early chop – waited for a solid setup.
✅ Break & retest gave conviction for the short.
✅ Trendline guided trade – locked profits before reversal.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Trade Recap – MSFT (March 6, 2025)
Strong Setup: BNR at ORH (5-min high) confirmed entry.
Entry: Long position after clean break & retest.
Momentum: Strong push up, filling premarket gap.
Trade Management: Followed PYA (Protect Your Asset) trendline.
Exit: Closed all contracts when price broke the PYA trendline.
Key Takeaways:
✅ Waited for confirmation – BNR setup worked.
✅ Premarket gap fill gave momentum for a strong move.
✅ PYA trendline guided the trade and secured profits.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Overview of Both Strategies:
My Strategy: I waited for the stock to clearly break above a crucial price level before entering, which typically signals that the stock might continue to rise. This is safer because it confirms the stock's upward momentum. I set a stop-loss close to my entry point to minimize potential losses and took profits as the price went up, adjusting my stop-loss to secure gains.

Your Strategy: UG (your)  entered the trade just after the stock retested an important level, without waiting for further confirmation of an upward trend. This approach can be riskier because it's uncertain whether the stock will keep rising after the retest. The stop-loss strategy wasn’t clearly defined, which might expose the trade to larger potential losses. You also entered after a significant price increase, which sometimes means the stock could pull back as traders start taking profits.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content: Key Difference: Market Conditions: My entry was based on a breakout strategy, which generally waits for the stock to prove it can stay above a significant level before entering. My friend’s approach was based on a retest strategy, which assumes the stock will hold the retested level without further confirmation. <@1336718908257730621>

Hey Lauren.

Green and Red lines are the top and bottom of Daily candles; he’s just looking a few days back, nothing too crazy unless there are not relevant levels close. All this in Daily chart. 

White lines are relevant levels or zones in the previous days. This is in the 1min chart.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
=======

Charting as Tony

Green = HTF level (above the current price when he marked the level)
Red = HTF level (below the current price when he marked the level)
White = Marked on previous day 1m level

author: unnas
content: I used to enter into these kind of trades when I was first learning about the break and retest strategy.  Every time I saw a good retest, I would take the trade (good thing it was only paper money back then).  The biggest lesson I learned is to develop a HTF thesis first, then enter a set up that lines up with the HTF thesis.  For example, we are currently in a downtrend and most stocks (including NVDA) are in a downtrend.  Therefore, a suggested setup would be to look for a shorting position on a key level.  I make sure to watch Jdubs premarket prep videos to get an idea of what to expect and what key levels to keep and eye on.  Trying to a Long position on a stock that is downtrending in the HTF is pretty risky.  Also, something new that I'm just learning is to use the QQQ and SPY price action to determine relative strength and weakness for the stock I'm considering to trade.  In a nut shell, the break and retest strategy works best when you pair it up with multiple confluences like HTF thesis, current market direction, and relative strength/weakness.  I'm very new to trading, but what' I'm learning so far is that simply applying the break and retest to a key level isn't optimal.  We need to start line up the strategy with multiple confluences.

author: Sam Nuho
content: Yea rookie mistake out of me, even though I had a bias towards downside for NVDA as market structure was bearish I kept telling myself to play both sides if the opportunity presents itself and not have a 'bias' which can go both ways I guess.

author: unnas
content: For me it was more about FOMO, that's why I kept taking every break and retest set up.  But now that I"m using real money, I'm focused on being picky and trying to make sure it's a trade Tony or Jdub would take.  I'm hoping to get lots of trades under my belt from their Live sessions before I get the confidence to try it out on my own.  Even if I don't enter their trades, just seeing how they think and what they see in real time is extremely rewarding.

Hi <@801169196015943731> and <@717554586843611217>,

Good evening, my mentors. I am a beginner currently going through the course, and I have completed four modules. I have a question regarding the concept of internal liquidity.

This concept states that when we observe equal highs or equal lows:

- **Equal Highs**: The price decreases, then returns upward to grab liquidity before rising again.
- **Equal Lows**: The price increases, then retraces downward to grab liquidity before falling again.

However, when I analyze the chart pattern, specifically the double bottom (which indicates equal lows), it appears that the price will rise. I have attached the BTC/USD chart from March 9, 2025, for reference, where the price indeed went down.

Could you please help clarify this for me? Thanks in advance!

I also posted this in the <#1222655586152480859> and <#1219022414239764520> channels, but unfortunately, I did not receive a response in either. I understand you are busy and truly appreciate your time. 

Thank you!

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
Your thesis was solid in terms of waiting for the 5-minute opening range and entering on the retest with a strong downclose candle. However, the execution timing was likely off.


Market Context Issue: The broader market looked to be moving up, which means you were taking a countertrend trade. This added risk to your setup.


5-Minute Range: As you pointed out, the range wasn’t in your favor. If the range is tight, there’s limited room for price to move cleanly.


Inside Ranging: I personally dislike trading when price is stuck inside a defined range (between key highs/lows) because:
There’s no clear breakout or momentum.

Price tends to chop around, making it hard to get a clean directional move.

Stop-loss placements become trickier due to unpredictable wicks.

Possible Fix: Consider waiting for a cleaner breakout or signs of market trend alignment before taking an entry.
Overall, great thought process, just need to refine entry timing and market alignment!

author: Tito Frescado
content:
Overview:
FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. I thought that since AMZN was above YDH so early in the day with a strong uptrend behind it on all timeframes that it might continue. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill. Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 

Setup Details:
Entry: FOMC day but strong uptrend on SPY and QQQ. AMZN showed a strong uptrend on all timeframes. AMZN was above YDH and retested with a strong hammer candle with long bottom wick. Entered on the close of the second hammer candle with long bottom wick, looking for the daily gap fill.
Position Size: Discuss the position size taken and why. Mention any adjustments made for risk management.
Exit: Exited when a candle closed below the first order bock. 
Profit/Loss: Lost 20 dollars on the trade. On second look, the was perhaps too optimistic trade on FMOC day when you expect the trade to stay within the PDR. Perhaps my entry was too aggressive because although the hammer was red and might have worked on another day, it was FMOC day. I should have waited for a green confirmation candle close above the red hammer that I entered on. It would have kept me out of the trade.

Rating: ⭐ [5 of Stars]
⭐ 1 Star: Although the setup was good. I should have waited for more confirmation that it wanted to continue above YDR.
Key Takeaways:
Lesson 1: Expect price to stay within YDR on FOMC day before 2:00. Wait for price to reach YDH or YDL looking for a quick scalp in the opposite direction
Lesson 2: Look for solid confirmation before entering a trade on FMOC day.
Missed Opportunities: No. the was a retest of the top of the daily gap but not enough room to move before hitting resistance at YDH.
Other Factors: FOMC

author: 305 Trader
content: hi Human. Here is what I can contribute with, nothing replaces price action of your current stock or symbol. Take a close look at the bottom tails of those bars. They were forming a new OB. Supply at lower prices by Sellers was not giving. So demand had to go higher in price to buy. This is why it went bullish. But, regardless, look always at your 15 min and 5 min tf always. If you do that, you do not really neee to mirror any other symbol other than your own chart. But learning market structure and price action is what does it.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok Human,  what I do always, is that I need to see confirmation on the 5 min tf and 15 tf, in the form of higher lows prior (for a long entry) or Lower highs (for a short entry) in both of these t/f prior my entry.  Its non negotiable. And always keeping track of where the move originated. Was it a rejection or a continuation from the PDH or PDL? This os why I do not trade in the 1st 30 mins of the market opening. Unless, the PM has clearly made these higher highs and lows formation I talked about.

author: 305 Trader
content: These A1 set ups occur throughout the day. You only need to let them come to you. And no FOMO if its quiet during your trade time. that is the main thing man, FOMO, oh its almost good, my rules are almost all good, then boom, I  get busted when I think is all “almost” all good with these rules.

author: 305 Trader
content: also, study everything about ChOCH and BOS concepts. They explain your price action next possible directional bias.

author: Lauren (lakatrades)
content:
<@808714606926495764> 
You likely entered too soon without proper confirmation. The entry candle barely closed above the 5-min low (491.03), and there was no strong follow-through. Price dipped back into the range quickly classic fakeout. I would've waited at least 1–2 more 1-minute candles to see if it held the breakout level as support. That retest would’ve given more conviction.

Also, this looks like chop zone behavior better to stay patient and let the setup fully form instead of jumping on the first break. You're thinking in the right direction though. Keep refining timing and confirmation.

author: Luke
content: what’s up <@1351067135899734046>, the narrative is on the 15min timeframe, and the entry and risk is on the 1min or 5min. I personally don’t take a lot of entries/manage risk on the 5min timeframe; I use the 1min timeframe majority of the time. But when it comes to the reaction around the key level, I wait for a strong 5min candle close off of that level showing the buyer or sellers stepping in. So if you’re entering a Break and Retest on the 5min timeframe, wait for a 5min candle close below your stop loss, and if you’re entering on the 1min timeframe wait for a candle close below your stop loss on the 1min. Hopefully that makes sense. The narrative is on the 15min, but the entries are on the 1min or 5min.

author: Tito Frescado
content:
I’m looking for critical feedback. If you see anything that I missed or could improve upon, don’t hesitate to criticize. Fire is nice, but I learn from your mistakes! Thanks!

SPY: I created 3 plans going into the day for SPY: 2 Pop and Fade scenarios, and one Dip and Rip in case the market wanted to correct (you never know). The price action fit my plan perfectly, however, I adjusted my short position graphic to reflect my actual entry point and better stop. I stopped out on the first trade when it showed the potential to reverse with a bar closing above the previous bar. Entered the second trade to grab the continuation move as it was within my original alternative short plan. I only had 2 contacts in each position. I managed on the 30s and 1m charts for entry, and the 1, 3 & 5 minute charts for holding and exits, looking for a 3 bar swing pattern to suggest reversal or an engulfing pattern for continuation.

author: 305 Trader
content: hi. Its due to Loss of momentum on your way down, and a momentum gain on the way up. Tony talks about loss of momentum in options trading. Increase your “R” as a suggestion to 1:2.

author: Kam
content: if trading options, have to accept some asymmetrical risk on both sides because of the impact of greeks on how the options are priced. Especially with the IV as high as it is on options pricing in the markets right now due to elevated volatility. This looks like the TSLA trade from a couple days ago - if trade is going sideways or against you the IV+theta will kill the options value very fast. The flip side to this is when you get a move in your favor, can make exponential / asymmetric returns also. remember, options value acccelerates the further price moves away fro your strike price. in this case, you didn't really get that aggressive acceleration on the down move - sort of chopped down. unfortunately on the move back up to your stop, the price did move fast in the wrong direction

author: thamerb777
content:
Thanks.
-Tuesday: Neutral. No direct impact from the Richmond Index, but it might move with QQQ. Hold off on trades until closer to earnings on Thursday.
-Wednesday: Markets may start positioning for Thursday’s earnings. If PMIs are strong and Waller is dovish, consider a small long position (calls) ahead of earnings. If data is weak, stay neutral or hedge (puts).
-Thursday: Avoid trading during the day unless economic data significantly impacts tech sentiment (e.g., strong Durable Goods Orders might support a pre-earnings rally). Post-earnings, if GOOGL beats (EPS > $2.01) and provides strong AI/Cloud guidance, go long for Friday. If it misses, short the initial drop or buy a dip if the long-term outlook remains solid.
-Friday: Post-earnings reaction dominates. If GOOGL beat expectations, expect a gap up—ride the momentum with a long position. If it missed, short the drop or buy a dip if the outlook is strong. Strong Consumer Sentiment could further support GOOGL.

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
In the upper left corner I have my Account info. Make sure you have the Complete display mode and there you can find your P/L and Settled cash to trade
Lower left corner is my Options list, I like to have separated Calls from Puts, but you can have them in the same list
Next to my Account info is the Watchlist, there you can choose what tickers to see so you can select the one you want to have in the Options list and in the Chart
Upper right corner are my 2 charts, always looking one individual name + QQQ
Positions tab displays when you have an open trade, something like this:

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
Also displays the Day P/L and the Amount value of your open position
Orders tab displays the working, filled, and all Orders. When you have a Working Order means you tried to Buy/Sell come Contracts but the Order hasn't filled yet. Filled Orders are the ones already filled and completed. All Orders are your history

Order Entry tab displays the Symbol, Strike, Expiration, Call/Put, Buy/Sell, Qty of Contract, , Order type (Limit, Market, etc), Price
.
.
.
Please let me know if you find this valuable and poke if you would like to discuss/comment anything

author: Luke
content: Wanted to share this trade I took on NVDA today. It was the 84% Rule of the 5min OR Low. I never entered on the first retest because there wasn't any confirmation candel for me to enter on. I entered at 10:13 EST, when I should never have because the first trade never got stopped out. What I should have done was entered at 10:30 EST after the first trade actually got stopped out, and it would have worked out nicely. LESSON: **Don't enter into an 84% Rule when the first trade didn't get stopped out; wait for the first trade to get stopped out first.** Hope some of you can relate to this🙂

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi JLD, I do not trade this stock though price action tells a story here. I am not sure if you also look at the 5 min and 15 min TF as context/advisory board for your entries in the 1 min TF? If you take a look at the time of where you took an entry is "no-bueno". Why? Both 15 min and 5 min Tf indicated a liquidity sweep from Sellers. They grabbed Buy side orders and down they went. When the mitigation to your POI is this violent, you gotta pay attention and weigh out a friction vs a valid retest. Is this friction on the way down or a valid retest? In any event, in this context I would wait for a confirmation candle, if the S/L ensuing is too high to chew, then I pass, or wait for more retest to my POI.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi Luke, this is quite subtle, yet it made me take a deep look at the consolidation it took place around your POI. I noticed there was a ChOCH in the 5 min t/f. When there is consolidation around  your POI for an entry, I swtich to the 5 min t/f and wait for L/H (PUTS) forming that can confirm my thesis for an entry. You had a ChOCH that induced Buyers to fill their orders. Only later, the market confirm breakdown after it gave a new L/H. See the graphs.

author: Haseeb Rehman
content:
DEMO TRADES!!!
Today was hectic for me. My first live trade after back testing for a couple weeks. Live trading is a whole different game. Made some blunders as I'm not used TWS. Took Tesla Calls instead of Puts and then took Tesla Puts at the wrong strike price. Cleared my head afterwards. However, since I missed the Tesla trade, I ended up taking a FOMO entry on NVDA. My three trades afterwards are as follows:
NVDA: Took a FOMO entry but did base it on the BnR of the 5min OR (The only setup I backtested). Ended up hitting my TP which was the PM Pivot Level. I knew there wasn't enough strength in QQQ for NVDA to go to PMH. It was a 3.47R trade.
AMD: I think it was a nice entry based on my setup. 5min ORH BnR with 1 candle rule. Closed my final position just before my last TP which was intraday resistance. It was a 4.37R trade.
QQQ: Wasn't a clean retest of the 5min ORH but with the 1 candle rule, I was able to take an entry of an impulsive candle. It was a 2.81R trade.
Please let me know what you guys think.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi. Going Long very near the HOD is always with a great risk of reversal or a serious pullback. Its similar to going short near the LOD. I always assume that near those levels a pullback is expected. Hence, for going long in your chart, I would near that bolded red line. And going short I would at the B&R of that bloded blue line. This is what is known also as "Buy in discount area" and "Sell (go short) in Premium area".

author: 305 Trader

author: 305 Trader
content: HI. I reviewed this set up just now. This set up served very well the 1st 15 min candle ORB strategy but to the d/s.  It did retest even twice the low. Now, only thing I can say against that CALL was that on the Daily chart the stock was overextended in relationship to the ADR of the previous 9 days. Context is so important. The stock opened up following a huuge gap, so I would have wanted to wait and observe what direction it would pick. There is this rule that I try reminding myself that says: Do not buy stock that is overextended to either side.  Oscar, buy CALLS in discount area and go short in Premium area. This simple yet tried and true notion can save you from FOMO and from doing a bad entry. Everything is possible, yes, but the odds are not in our favor when we buy when the stock or index is overextended.

author: Kam
content:
Hi Chris — appreciate you sharing this one, solid context and nice work calling out the thesis. A few nuances you might find helpful on review:

1. Context check – NVDA was trading inside the prior day’s range, so it’s a tougher environment for longs unless the setup is really clean. In those conditions, any play often needs to be treated as a scalp unless something really breaks open.

2. Initial strength – The push above OR was nice, but keep an eye on the repeated upper wicks near 143.14 (prior pivot from yesterday). That level seemed to act as early resistance.

3. Trigger candle sequence – The 9:45 hammer is a good eye, but watch how 9:46 fails to follow through — another upper wick and no strong body. That’s often a red flag in this kind of setup. Remember what Tony/Jdub say about “want to see strong PA here” — that second candle needs to prove it.

4. Opening wick awareness – That big wick on the opening candle turned nito OB is worth clocking. Even though the 9:35 hammer dug into it, it wouldn’t be unreasonable for price to come all the way back toward 142.50 and still be structurally intact. That should factor into your risk and target structure.

Hope this is helpful!

author: Kam
content:
I was watching this one too - looked like a really solid trade. You had stronger PA here than on the NVDA retest, and the context was cleaner with the PDH reclaim plus that tight cluster of levels (PDH, PMH, OR) stacking right below.

Only real consideration: this was right before 10:00, and even though there was no red folder news today, that time can occasionally bring a bit of chop or whipsaw - especially on Fridays. Just something to stay mindful of when timing entries that close to the top of the hour.

But overall, this looks like a very valid trigger with conviction behind it

author: Kam
content:
You had a solid thesis here, but as a wise man <@1014389514141184074> once told us, "never trust AAPL" 😩 

But in seriousness, but your own reflection maybe nails the potential miss: SPY gave clearer structure, and AAPL may have been the laggard catch-up play that felt available. Imo structurally, AAPL’s rejection wicks on the pullback sequence and broader daily range put it in a tougher spot for continuation despite the opening drive. I don't think you were wrong to try it, but the momentum wasn’t clean - and the HTF still had unresolved congestion. Good eyes on the review 💪

author: Kam
content:
Kuba - first off, solid work on the entries across the board. You’re reading levels really well and triggering on logical reclaim/retest sequences. Nicely done!

The challenge, as you already know, isn’t setup quality - it’s your exit conviction.  I totally get that the “1 con” situation definitely makes things feel more fragile, but something to consider from these trades is that perhaps your trade management is reacting to price flickers, not to your original plan or RR zones. Hindsight is always 20/20 but perhaps something to ask yourself is how would I have handled scales if I had 2-3 cons?

author: Kam
content:
I think these are further evidence you’re not missing the setups. You’re not even mistiming entries. You’re simply not giving these trades the room to breathe through your target zones. And while the 1-con constraint makes that harder, the deeper issue may be that your exit logic is driven by discomfort rather than invalidation.

All three trades had valid structure. All three showed follow-through. And all three were closed early out of fear. If you want to level up without changing your contract size, consider defining hard structural targets ahead of time - and only exit if those are invalidated.

Keep working on the exits (this is probably the weakest part of my game also) Your entries and eye for structure look fantastic!

author: Ruslan /ruˈslan/
content:
Hi Kuba, I think I have the same issue as you, and maybe my experience can help. I also trade with just one contract (due to a small account) and often end up with small profits because I chickened out and exited too early — or I hesitated to sell, price dropped, and I took a loss instead of securing a small green.
I asked <@1014389514141184074> for advice on the best strategy for trading with a single contract — maybe it'll help you too. He said: “Set your TP at 1:1.5 RR, and once price goes past it, turn that level into your stop-loss and see if it continues.” Though I see you're already going for 1:2 RR, which is great.
But here's something I realized this past week: it’s not really about  "chickened out" vs "had the balls", it’s about trading a single contract with no ability to scale out, and I'll explain why. Despite my small account, I decided to try trading two contracts this week — and man, it’s a whole different game. Trading before and after this shift feels like night and day.
That inner conflict is gone — no more one voice saying “hold for more profit” while the other says “just take what you can before it turns red.” Now I calmly wait for TP1, take partial profit, or get stopped out — either way, it feels in control. And when you lock in some gains at TP1, it’s so much easier to let the rest ride toward TP2 with a peaceful mind.
Another great tip I got from <@801169196015943731> — which I now use — is after scaling out at TP1, move your stop to breakeven. That way you protect your profits and keep your risk low.
Sure, sometimes price reverses right away and it’s a small red — but I liked what <@1014389514141184074> said yesterday on trade review — that’s still a win. Because you followed your plan, didn’t lose control, and didn’t blow half your account 😄 We're not aiming for a 100% win rate anyway.
So my advice: try adding that second contract. It changes everything.

I personally would because of my risk amount. I would be waiting for a full retest. Your stop loss in the image is sort of in the middle of nowhere. it would be completely reasonable for the trade to come back down and fully retest the 5 min OR +  the one candle rule at 9:34. If price then closes slightly below the 5 min ORB you would be stopped out but Nvidia's structure would still be in tact for a move up.

Size down and give the trade enough room so that it can pull back into the 5 Min ORB and not stop you out until it breaks market structure.

author: jest1
content:
Hey Chris, just looking through this channel and saw this post from a couple of days ago about the 2 different green candles. I nerd out on technicals and wanted to offer an explanation (bear with the length of the reply)

While both candles show buyer strength and potential bullish reversal (especially at the bottom of a downtrend), the second candle is considered stronger. 

It's based on the wicks. The 2nd candle is a hammer (small body at the top of a long lower wick and little/no upper wick)

The long lower wick shows the stock traded (significantly) lower than its opening price during the candle period (I don't know what length of time is being shown) but buyers rallied to close on top, so there was buying pressure during the candle period to push price up.

The first candle isn't quite an inverted hammer (for hammers the wick should be ~2x the length of the body), but the "wick tells the story" part as we say here shows that buyers were initially strong and tried to push price up but couldn't close at those higher prices. Sellers pushed price back down, leaving the upper wick.

author: Kam
content:
Took a pretty lousy trade on PLTR this morning and wanted to share my review. Open to any thoughts or other feedback! 

PLTR – 6/26/25
Setup: BnR 1m OB Continuation + 5m OR Retest
Result:  Loss (–1R)
Confidence on Entry: 3/5
Sizing: Base Size

What Happened:
Strong opening drive in PLTR with RS and news catalyst. Took an anticipatory reclaim entry off the 1m OB after visual strength near prior pivot, but skipped confirmation and entered on candle stretch. Market context was improving, 5m candle looked like a nice hammer, but pullback was steep and PLTR can be trappy. Trade hit stop shortly after entry. Earlier missed trade off PMH may have been a factor as well as pass on NVDA OB play prior day.

Classification:
False Form – Anticipatory Entry 
Good structure idea, poor entry execution. Trade plan shifted from OB pullback to reclaim without re-evaluation.

Primary Mistake:
Premature Entry – No Reclaim Confirmation

What Worked:
- Avoided forced dip buys in semis
- Honored stop and respected system
- Solid structure read on RS and catalyst

Needs Work:
– Entry discipline on reclaims
– Slight emotional residue from passing on NVDA prior day and PMH retest at the open
– OB sequence clarity and reclaim filters need reinforcement

author: Jatin
content: Hi, I am looking to get some feedback on the trade I took this morning. Entered in at 9:57 candle with confluences with QQQ and SMH with 2 cons. 1st profit target at HOD and was then following strictly order blocks for continuation with the last con but stopped out at 10:07 candle. I did not wait for the candle closure below $155.60 but exited on the wick as I wanted to avoid any losses that was a mistake I realize. Anything else that I could have done differently. Any feedback is much appreciated.

author: Kam
content: Nice trade Lando! Agreed that the PDH sweep and steep rejection should have been a bigger red flag on the overall setup quality

author: Kam
content:
Hey Jatin - this is a tricky one for me since it's lower timeframe bearish, but HTF bullish. I didn't take this trade, but here's how I hypothetically would have managed it:

The 155 area was the reclaim level after the 5m hammer into the PDH level so once we get a candle close above that level I'd draw out the full OB and use that as structural invalidation. I'd have taken profit like you at HOD. That nasty wick held that OB - and after taking profits at HOD, the trade is de-risked so if it comes back it comes back, and stop out for BE or small loss depending on your size. I probably would have sold into new high pops at 156.5, but these setups usually work fast and hit PMH quickly. So once it stalled out, I probably would have been cautious when got back under the HOD pivot and 1m/5m OB to stop out of trailers. 

There's different ways to trail though - I tend to use OB's and structure to trail, but others like <@1121621751764369501> use trendlines and EMAs. <@1014389514141184074> uses a 5m timer! Just have to find your style!

Easy to play armchair QB with hindsight bias i know 😂  But that's my 2c, thanks for asking - this was a good thought exercise!

I know Tony & Jack took this trade cos they were just talking about it in Traders Lab - so definitely check out Tony's trade review of this tonight!

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content:
As part of my Performance Coaching Program, let me share one of my favorite tools to stay sharp while I’m in a trade: a simple 5-minute timer.

I started using this because I noticed that when I wasn’t checking in regularly, emotions could sneak in and take over my decisions — hesitation, fear, frustration, or even overconfidence. So I added this timer to help me stay present, focused, and objective during the trade.

Every 5 minutes, it reminds me to check in with my position — not to overreact, but to stay connected and aware. I ask myself:
    •    Is my stop loss or invalidation level still valid?
    •    Are we getting closer to my targets?
    •    What’s happening with price action and market structure?
    •    Is there any news, correlation, or divergence that could affect the trade?

This timer is now one of my tools to overcome emotional decision-making. It’s like a little coach tapping me on the shoulder every few minutes saying:
“Hey, are you still managing this trade intentionally?”

Try it out — you might be surprised how much clarity and confidence it can bring to your sessions.

author: MrGmint

author: Neto Moreno (Performance Coach)
content: Turned on after my entry. Again, this is just a reminder to stay connected to your Trade intentionally. If something on your questions is not aligned to your idea then you manage your position accordingly, if your trade is actually going in the right direction according to your plan then you don't panick and start selling just because. This is just a tool to stay sharp all time

content: This is the mental trap I have been falling into and loosing big, way more than what i am making while winning, Even i knew I had to exit, some how convincing myself at all key level to stay in the loss making trade and adding more to avg down. author: Dee
content: You followed your plan on the first trade. It was after that it all went side ways. META just made new ATH and on higher time frames it was bullish, therefore it should not be a high probability short. Regarding overcoming averaging down what I am doing is to reframe what a winning trading day is for me. If I average down and I win I didn’t win that day and the profit is just a loan the market will eventually take back if I continue to break my rules. I think you should journal when you start to tilt. Is it after you take a loss or when you take a trade without a stop loss in mind before entering the trade?. I am still working through averaging down. It is not easy, give yourself some grace to overcome

author: 305 Trader
content: hi. Its called Loss aversion, your emotions are protecting your need to be right, and you try hard to be right cause admitting loss is failure in our wiring. But being a trader is about accepting loss from the get-go. You can only control your loss, the market gives you wins when it wants to. This is an explanation I repeat myself over and over as I struggled with your same problem for years. No adding though, but loss aversion yes. Watch few videos on trading mindset in youtube, I know one great channel on psychology of trading but its in spanish. Two things, try mechanical stop losses while you work your psychology, this means do only A+ set ups, two trades at most, and cut down in size.

author: invest2reinvent
content: Thank you <@1080561490576285706>, I will check on your points. I was placing SL orders right away. But many times SL was hit and SL price  was the lowest before returning in opposite direction. So I thought to observe the price action and sell when it’s really time to exit. Then going down that slippery slope. So Yes placing the SL before hand protected the down side many times. 👍

author: 305 Trader
content: ok, remember this is a mind battle. If you have found your edge in this community of Jack and Tony, that is number 1. Once you found your edge and make sure you have one cause you only need one strategy, not many, submit it to sample 20 trades with a mechanical stop loss perhaps 10-20% of your contracts value (depending on contracts size) being your risk. If after 20 trades you have been always stopped out, then your edge needs to be revised or your entry timing adjusted. Journal this. Also, what matters is your profit factor, not your win ratio.

author: VK
content: Hi guys. I just want to share my own "10 ALWAYS Trading Rules" here. Maybe someone finds it useful.

author: VK
content:
TRADING RULES


Alway wait for A+ setup of Break & Retest
Always watch the market trend before entering.
Always follow Risk Management rules.
Always risk no more than 1-2% of an account.
Always have my STOP LOSS in place.
Always trade 1 contract ONLY.
Always make no more than 1-2 trades a day.
Always STOP trading after 1 losing trade.
Always avoid FOMO, GREED, REVENGE trades at all cost.
Always track my trades and emotions by journaling.

author: 305 Trader
content:
hi Francine. I thought of making a review to your trade, because I found it pretty good a teaching for me. You got the direction right, as you can see Apple had been on a upward ride for days. So, yes momentum had been there, thats 1. Now as for your entry, since AAPL demonstrated that the uptrend was clear for several days, that bounce from your wedge in purple lines was your POI and not the ORB you drew. Additionally, the break out from that wedge had confluence with one of your KL. This was a powerful event you should have not missed.  If I was to take a swing or even a day trade on AAPL, it would have been on the break and retest (in a smaller tf) of that confluence. In the higher timeframe (swing) this was also, the continuation signal of the market in its uptrend. So, your entry was late when you decided to make it off of that ORB way above. 
Or, if taking the swing off of that ORB, it would need to be after the mitigation with a strong rejection to that POI or ORB you drew. I think you took it on the 1st impulse instead  and hence you had the expected pullback. I am curious how the symbol did on Friday since I had not traded since last Tuesday.

author: Royal191
content:
Hey <@268284125897687041> At a high level view, I would say slow down and focus on 1-2 trades per day. If you took 2 Ls there is no need to continue and possibly end up with 4 or 5 Ls. Just call it a day and review what went wrong and come back the next day with a fresh view. Overtrading and revenge trading is a big reason why traders are not profitable because your losing days become bigger than your winning days.
Based on what you said it seems like you might be chasing trades or anticipating moves. You should spend some time and refine your system rather than trying out different strategies or entry models. (if you haven’t watched the accelerator videos yet, I would highly recommend you do so).
Also based on your charts it looks like some of your trades are kinda taken in the middle of no where, away from your levels with the exception of  the TSLA entry. On the TSLA trade by the way I can see a V shape recovery on your chart before you entered short.  You said it yourself on the TSLA trade you were anticipating the move. Watch the accelerator modules. Try to plan your trades in the pre-market and focus on those and wait for entries/reaction at your key levels. If you need help coming up with a plan for the day and main watches. Join Jack’s pre-market prep, over time you will learn how to read the HTF, how to draw key levels and much more. Check out the old trade reviews from Jack and Tony. Easiest way to learn what they look for and when they enter trades. https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1219022089252503632 and https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222648559682457650

author: Sean
content:
GM, Looking for a bit of feed back with my PM Prep. 

The SPY  looks like it has some potential continuation. I see two opportunities to enter. One at the retest of the PDH  and another at the retest at the PMH. Which would be the best one to choose and why? Would the PMH be better to select because the PDH is in the middle of the H/L of the PM?

author: Luke
content:
I think the Previous Day High is a higher probability level in itself because everyone can see it one their chart, while the premarket high was made with low volume and not everyone is looking at it.
In this case I would use Previous Day High if there was a nice entry to enter off of it, but if price decided to rip straight up, then you could use Premarket High since it's a higher up level, and pair it with a confluence like an Order Block or Fair Value Gap if you want. I like the thesis!

author: Luke
content: Nice job for following your rules and stopping out when you should that's awesome! But I would set the stop loss at the break of the 9:36 candle high, since that was the impulsive candle that broke your key level. I can see why you would have yours at the break of structure on the 1min timeframe, but Tony and Jack taught me to put it at the break of the impulsive candle. You can also see on the 5min timeframe that it never broke structure to the upside and actually was making a Lower-High where you stopped out. Hopefully that helps

author: Sean
content: Yeah, I like this. This was what I was thinking as well, but was too busy wondering why tony put down PMH instead of PDH. I normally trade off of PDH. Thanks for the response back, it's going to help me in the future.

author: 305 Trader
content: Here is a trick I apply. I do not enter at or near HOD or LOD. I wait for the price to retrace back to a POI, and then on  the B&R of my POI in continuation to the market direction, I take an entry, then my 1st TP will HOD or LOD, and let it ride.

author: wakefieldcapital
content: Interesting. Any examples of a valid POI that you look into?

author: 305 Trader
content: yes. Certainly, a POI is a “point of interest” you watch on PA. In your graphic it was right at where Luke said. $196.5. Most of the time there is a pull back at HOD or LOD. This I  why I never enter near there. By doing this, I keep control of a tight stop loss.

author: Luke
content: on your first trade, after seeing that harsh red candle I would have your hand on your mouse ready to sell; although it didn’t close below your stop loss i’d still stop out as it starts to move down past your stop loss, since sellers are very strong based on the candle at 10:09. Stopping out where you did would make your loss just as big as a win. Nice win on the 2nd trade!

author: Papa
content: In general there should have been more displacement off of the PMH level for it to be considered a higher probability retest, expect reaction there with sellers which is what happened.  The entry for retest would have been a better entry off the first 5 min high hammer candle for low risk, 1 min chart has multiple one's there with buyers stepping in.  Also something to keep in mind, the 5 min chart shows 3 green bars, that kind of move usually requires a retracement, things like to move in 3's.  The reclaim of the PMH was an aggressive entry, would have been better to wait for HOD B/R with displacement to confirm continuation higher.  Hope this helps <@1070052905522053272>

author: Royal191
content: Nice trade. Nothing wrong with protecting your gains. I think what you have to be able to do for yourself is differentiate between your scalps and a day trades. So if you are scalping you lock in your profits quickly at strategic levels or on big pops selling into the strength. On day trades with a strong thesis you hold you trades through the pullback aiming for the HTF TPs. Took me a while to figure this out. I was stuck in scalper’s mode for a long time, always stopping out on pullbacks on my day trades.

author: Sean
content: Thanks for the feed back. I think that's what i need to do is have a better understanding of which days are going to be scalps and which can be day trades. Any ideas how you were able to get past that?

author: Royal191
content: Screen time and experience pretty much. With time and journaling you will figure out what works for you and what dont. FOMC days for one is usually a lower probability day so that’s a scalping day for me or no trade. I scale more aggressively on scalps. Also if I’m taking an intra-day trade against the overall trend it’s a usually a scalp. It mainly depends on the higher time frame, the overall trend and what the indices are doing. Watch module 7 & 8 if you havnt yet or re-watch im sure you will pick up something new. Check out this vid as well. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oNyFOBIKERo&t=461s

author: f.lo
content:
Hi <@268284125897687041>! I am a noob here. But from what I can see that may be preventing you from winning are:

1) you may have too many indicators going on that you may be complicating things. 
2) yes, IMO, that day you over traded and it looks like you revenge traded as your trades were so close together. Also looks like you were chasing not “reacting” to PA. Also consider patiently waiting for your step up  of the B&R. 
3) I’m unclear on your strategy you’re using. Are you using trendline, B&R, or the combination of  both mixed in with some confluence from the other indicators? 
3) SPDR trendline should have been to the upside. It was forming  HHs and HLs.  PA was  consolidating to understand if it was bearish or not. 
4) TSLA trade 1 no visible B&R that would make me think it was bearish. Mark the KLs. Trade 2 there was a break but no retest. 
5) Need to also mark out your KLs for PMH/PML, OR HL for 5M & 15M. Tony goes into detail about this in one of his recent videos. 

I hope this helps!

author: Sean
content:
Update: I was able to successfully stick to my risk management plans and continuously improve it throughout the month. 

Some statements that have helped me stick to the plan are:

- There is no certainty in trading—only probabilities. 
- Acknowledge that a loss is simply one outcome in a long series of trades. 
- I can always look to reenter the trade if something sets up

Plans for Next Month: Same thing as last Month, Respect my stop loss. Search for points of invalidation in my trades before entering and respect that stop no matter what. 

Hopefully, this helps anyone struggling with the same challenges. I'm right there with you. We'll get there. One trade at a time. Size wisely, place stops with purpose, and stay disciplined. Consistency beats perfection. Keep showing up — we’ve got this. 🥂 💪
