author: John H
content:
Quick post here regarding my oversized losses from Friday's session. I decided I would play WDC, and as we witnessed memory names had a brutal selloff. But more importantly what did I do wrong. I came in simply unprepared. WDC is not a ticker I normally trade. I had zero premarket prep. No key levels to watch and no exit plan when the trade turned against me. I failed to check a higher time frame to see that we were selling off. I should have marked off the open high low on the 5 min. candle as is often discussed here and I didn't do that either. So, an entire solid month of very good trading cost me more than 1/2 my gains for January. 

To make matters worse, I continued to trade. While I won most of those trades, I took yet another big loss on SNDK. I entered very late chasing more downside and the stock reversed near end of day. I'm saying this as a reminder to myself and for others that might be newer traders how very important it is to map out your game plan. To stay disciplined. To know your exit point if the trade turns against you. To not overtrade by taking too many trades, or revenge trade to try and recoup losses. Had I followed <@1014389514141184074> Friday advice I would of had a much better week & month. I was up $42K for January, I ended with $16K. I was disciplined all month long until Friday. That is truly how quickly you can make careless errors and give it all back. 

I still have a very high win rate, but you'll find that does not matter if you let trades get worse by the minute and don't get out when they are clearly not working. I will come to the market prepared, or I will not trade. That is something I have to follow for myself. We ride again Monday, and you all will see me turn this around. Best of luck everyone.

author: John H
content: One final thought. I would like to thank <@801169196015943731> for the amount of effort he puts into this discord. The daily premarket prep, the staying a few minutes after each trading session to answer questions. The detailed trade reviews win or lose. The time he dedicates to us on Saturdays to review trades and answer questions. This is a true coach & mentor we are lucky to have. I have not tuned in to a Saturday session in a while, and something today was telling me I should. I heard a lot of things I needed to hear again about trade management, sizing risk, and having a gameplan. Appreciate you Jack!

author: Fred
content:
Hi, here my trade for today. The result wasn't very good : two losses (-2.7R) and one win (+2R).
What's your opinion on the process ?

In 1h TF, we broke and retested the previous high from last Wednesday. Last Thursday, we broke the Wednesday low, which we then retested on Friday. The plan today was either a continuation of the bearish trend (break of the red zone/PDL) or a rejection of the previous low/PDH (red zone).

In 15m TF I identified 2 potential areas for a reverse (green area, which coincides with the red zone for rejection in 1h TF) and 1 area for continuation (red area).

On the 1m TF, the scenario is bullish; we disregard the bearish continuation opportunity and focus on the reversal. Based on my key zones identified on the 1-hour and 15-minute charts, I took puts on breakouts and retested previous 1-minute highs (market structure/yellow area).

author: Luke
content: Hey <@293563891345129472>, I really like the trade based on a "5min setup." As far as momentum on the 1min timeframe, you would like to have seen it work out right away rather than consolidating. After about 10:12am EST, I would have not been interested in it anymore based on the 1min timeframe since you would like to see it reject immediately especially for shorts. This is where you could go to the 5min timeframe if you have more of a bearish thesis and look to enter based off of the 5min timeframe. Also, another reason this would be a "5min setup" (you'll hear Jack and Tony refer to these as that) is you can see that there is a lot of displacement... in my opinion just a little too much for momentum shorts on the 1min timeframe for me, but based on the 5min chart it looks super nice. But besides that, your stop loss looks solid and your entry. This was definitely a trade you could have looked to re-enter with the 84% Rule on. Nice trade though!!

author: Joebag009
content:
The numbers: Cash account +64  Margin account:  -400
Take Profit Trader eval: -162 Top Step eval: -283 Apex evals: No trades

The Story
Lots of learning today. I’m not doing well personally, or in trading at the moment. Spilling my guts on here in a narrative others can comprehend instead of just short form writing to myself might help me work through it. I have done much better before so I know I can do this. 

Cash account: I have been trading in a margin account and sizing up worked great until my slump, since I have been trading 1-3 cons lately, I figured why not try a cash account with no margin? I took 720 bucks and put it in a cash account. I did one quick trade with JDUB on 2 0 day cons of NVDA and made 64 bucks. I could have held longer for continuation – I have gotten better, but still hold losers longer than winners. Losses happen faster than winners, cutting needs to be ruthless. I got lots of insight so my new rule for next week is trade 1 account only – cash if I can, margin if the trade of the day needs more capital. 

Margin account: I then had a thesis of continuation and a quick win in my margin account, but instead of waiting for a break and retest of that low of day on NVDA I entered too soon and it turned out being low of day. I stopped out on 2 NVDA 0 days for a loss of 314. Re-entering with hope of continuation and being wrong is something I am seeing as a problem. I will need to stick to the original setup and once I’m out, I’m out. If it costs me, I need to get better at exiting, instead of trying to re-enter. I also need to keep in mind 0 days move different. Looking at Fridays for the last 3 months I don’t see a meaningful performance difference from other days, so for now I’ll keep trading them.

author: Joebag009
content:
I took a quick apple scalp since JDUB was looking at it and bought two 0 day cons. One sold at profit of 17 and one tight stop out at -9. They reversed shortly after so my exit was ok. Then I saw NVDA come back within 5 bucks of where I entered. It retested and I tried again with Jack and chat talking about continuation (but not high conviction on retrospect, it didn’t close under the level I was looking at with conviction and the market reversed). While I was wrong on the setup I did manage risk by switching from 0 days to next Friday expiry. I had a tighter stop but sized up like an idiot to 3 cons instead of sticking to 1 on a lower probability setup and overall should have stayed out because it is Friday and my 2nd attempt at NVDA already failed. Looking back it felt rational but was an emotional trade. I lost another 118 in my margin account stopping out. 

I took a solo trade going long on QQQ when it popped above the 15 minute orb and defended it with the 10:31 candle, it did not continue higher so I sold my single 0 day con for a profit of $33.00 very quickly. This turned out to be a smart decision as it didn’t break higher for two more hours, by then most would have stopped out and any contract would have decayed.

I gave spy long a try but had an ultra tight stop and was done at -9 bucks, also wise as it rejected that level about 10 times and then failed bigly hours later.

author: Joebag009
content: I had a big AHA that if it was my cash account I never would have risked subsequent NVDA trades, so I think I might be trading my cash account with 720 in it (and taking profit out each day) for a few days to build my psychology up with a fresh start. Another big aha is that with 0s and even generally the first trade is the best trade and unless I have a new thesis or proved continuation (preferably with divergence from the market), re-entering is a bad idea - looking at my trading notes I have had wins in the past on re-entering for continuation, especially in big dumps around key levels and in grinds but this week it has not worked out (as in net profit) and eaten my meagre profits. Instead of blaming the market, I am adding new habits of pausing between trades and to reload I will develop a quick checklist for myself - did it defend a key level and continue or is it just teasing me, or is it just teasing me as it mirrors qqq moves (hey, it got Jack with TSLA this week, I need to be kind to myself, but also prevent it next time), is it over-extended on the stock, and with qqq overall? What is the daily tf trend? Are Jack and Tony still in and either not at target or just tp1? Tp2 and “to the moon” are horrible places for re-entry, even if it is holding a key level or 1 minute timeframe. Reminder to myself that re-entries pay less, fail more often, and lose money more quickly than initial setups.

author: Joebag009
content:
Jack and Tony signed off for the day so I switched my attention to FlowZone on Youtube my Take Profit Trader (TPT) and Topstep evals with mnq micros. Lots of trades where take profit and stop were within a range of 20 bucks to get out of the trade as the scalp setup failed. On TPT my eval is almost blown from being on tilt the other day, I went from 248 of wiggle room down to 104 so I paused on that account and hope to trade back on an A+ setup later or on a different day. I was careful and followed my rules, just had the trade go against me. I tried one more trade later and was wrong, down to 86 in the eval, maybe I’ll get lucky next week and build it back up. If I blow it I will focus on my other evals. I tried a few props and the number I have now is too much to trade. My favorite overall is TPT-reasonable cost, love tradeovate online, not as rule heavy as Apex.

On topstep I ground to +150 then down to 50 then ranged 60 to 120 and took a pause as the market just couldn’t decide what it wanted and scalping volatility died down and I wanted to protect my hard won gains. I then broke my rule of being done when the streamer is and went on tilt, shut off the chart at -283 so I can live to fight next week.

author: Joebag009
content:
I keep getting to the half way point of passing evals and then getting close to blowing them, grinding up, then either failing with tight margin or a second tilt day. I wish I had tried props sooner, days of learning only costs me the eval fee. I wish more that I was passing them. I want to try trading multiple accounts at the same time with a copy trader for base hits that add up, I have two Apex accounts and wanted to link them to Ninja trader, only to find out that the feature was removed with the latest update to Ninja 8. I might try trading both together on Rthmic trader pro, but haven’t figured out the right settings and also need to tweak the master settings to switch from Ninja to Rthmic. I was super exited for Ninja with all the flashy Youtube shorts out there, it looks like ass out of the box and I am let down. So far my best results have been with TPT. Apex trailing drawdown scares me, I need 1 solid trade per day which might be good for my trading, but if I trail too close I’ll get stopped out in profit and then if I try to re-enter I’ll get cooked on small moves and spreads. If I size up I can’t give it too much room without blowing the account. Two micros and one trade attempt per day might be the best way to go, but how much is a healthy stop and healthy trail for Apex. I think Flowzone has a video on Apex best trading practice so I’ll check it out. I need to start trading the accounts next week so I can pass before they renew but this week has sucked and I’m not starting on a Friday.

Between all my accounts my challenges are: overtrading, tilt, sizing up instead of being consistent, not differentiating between good and crap setups, and rule breaking by staying on charts too long.

author: Joebag009
content: Wednesday I reviewed again all my Feb trades. I would be green in all my accounts this month if I just followed my rules. This means I need to shift focus from working on my trades and rules to working on myself. This would be a good time to mention my adhd brain makes trading an emotional roller coaster, even with meds and living with the diagnosis over a decade now. My goal today was to shut off the computer when streamers were done (no solo trades anymore until I get my mojo back as in profitable again). I broke it and it hurt me again, so I will do more inner work this weekend to figure out how my rational brain keeps getting circumvented even with rules and repeated failure.

content:
Hey <@708108045301055548>, I see what you were going for here. I see a few things here: I like that 5min low for a move down to HTF key levels. It looks like you entered at 10:06am EST on the reclaim of Premarket Low. Before that though, you can see that I have a level from the 15min timeframe (not a super strong level but good for context). You can see it actually swept it beforehand and bounced off of it, so for me I would need to wait for a reclaim or retest of the 179.45; it looks like you didn't have that key level drawn so in your case the the context is pretty good.
One other small thing you can see is there was a lower-high made at 9:57am EST and it was actually broken at 10:03am EST and the 1min timeframe technically had already reversed before you entered, and was now creating a higher-low.
Those are just some small things that stick out to me. Overall solid trade idea though. You can see on the HTF that NVDA has a lot of room down if it can get below Premarket Low/1790.45 HTF key level and has room down to at least Feb 6th's LOD. Hope that helps

author: Joebag009
content: Could have entered a bit more agressivly based on market movement, but I say that right now with hindsight, at the time who knows right? You could have given it another candle of room and it would have worked out. Or waited for the close of a continuation candle to enter.  I don't think you missed anything, but from that position it's not an a+ setup since there was already a move with continuation above your entry.

author: Joebag009
content:
Prop accounts: TPT -about 30 and 50 cents away from blowing it, will try a trade but fully expect to lose it and not planning to renew – will focus on my other accounts now that I have ruled out trading outside NY session.

TopStep: no trades.

Apex 02: +105 followed rules well
Apex 01: -264 slightly overtraded but caught myself and closed the charts before it got worse

Elite athletes don’t rise to the occasion; they fall to their level of training. -Neto Moreno

Theme of the day and Aha #1: SLOW THE HELL DOWN, be patient, DO NOT LONG OR SHORT IN CHOP, AT EDGE OF RANGES, AT HIGH OR LOW OF DAY… so in other words looking at a 1/5/15 min TF of a daily chart, the move looks HUGE, it makes us GREEDY, it makes us FEEL LIKE A BAD TRADER for “only” capturing xyz… but really, “perfect” is timing to SAFELY capture a relatively TINY part of that move with HIGH expectancy and CONSISTENCY. Doing it with 0s like JT (Jack and Tony) grants extra rewards.

Big aha for me today is how much more 0s can move compared to Friday expiries, 80% vs 20 profit roughly, however 0s are sooo spicy, even 1 option feels so high risk, definitely with the low discipline I have had lately. So, I could do 1 con 0 and be out when in profit, no re-entries ever on 0s, just with longer expiries, and not to mention that I’m super cooked if JT are wrong. I feel like I am at the beginning of my trading journey again, learning how to execute accurately and repeatedly with a single 0 will be a new challenge for me, I understand these are just for the main trade of the day and an A+ setup, I will want more wiggle room on most days until I’m consistently green.

author: Joebag009
content:
Struggle I identified last week was juggling too much at once: evals with MNQ and FZ (FlowZone), options with JT. It is too many accounts. My original thesis was options in the AM followed by MNQ all day, it is 24/5 after all. For almost a month now I tried trading globex, I tried Asia and London, I tried open (5 central, Sunday afternoon etc.) and my results are mixed – very much like my early trading journey with medium wins and big losses wiping it all out, plus mental exhaustion, replicated in the youtubers I watch who trade all these sessions. One clean setup over and over, like Neto talks about often, and JT implicitly in their Youtube vids. Overtrading for me boils down to: desperation, greed, fear, poor impulse control, and revenge trading. I have conquered them individually, but need to be ready for any to pop up randomly depending on my emotional state and how the day is going. 

Today I did okay with options and then shifted my focus to futures which prevented overtrading and losses by maintaining focus on one thing at a time, however now I’m worried I won’t pass evals without sustained focus on a big move. I will need to be disciplined if trades don’t line up to protect gains, let some evals fail, and perhaps focus a morning just on futures – there is more of a grind in props but much lower cash risk compared to options with my own money – one con of options is the same as like 3 evals or more if they are on sale, however it is a journey to get to profitability with props.

author: Joebag009
content:
Big aha with FZ today is that just like with JT I overtraded and didn’t quite match his execution. My goal today was to trade less and more focused on his stops and setups. While I missed some of the moves and botched the execution a bit, I at least paid attention to him with a new type of mindset and followed through and am up in 1 out of two Apex accounts. I could feel my frontal cortex straining, doing REPS, as I breathed through my emotions and focused on market structure. I stayed OUT of MULTIPLE trades, that WOULD NOT HAVE WORKED. So that was a huge psych win for me today, which I need cause I feel like I’m on the cusp of either making it big or completely losing my mind. FZ did 3 trades today, I did more with less size, same pattern as with JT and re-entries, but a bit easier managed on the charts so less friction. 

12:30 PM Eastern behaviour of futures seems to change a bit and most consistent pros are done here if not a lot sooner. Big AHA: a price move on a chart is not equal, cons and futures move different depending on price volume and market structure. “d’uh” but seeing it in real time is different than seeing it in a lesson. A “choppy/grindy” move in both futures and contracts is a lot crappier than a clean smaller move with strong volume and direction. Again “d’uh”, but starting to see it is making a big difference in my emotions and execution. Also after 12:30 fills get worse on stopping out and taking profits making it harder to stack up wins that recover from even small losses (because bad fills make them even 3x worse than AM exit with the same distance stop). In other words, what the chart shows is a lie, the risks of extracting money from what you see go UP.

author: Joebag009
content:
Low of day to VWAP is a great secondary setup for futures, not usually for options as they move differently. Maybe I could capture that move on an index weekly, but I’m usually busy trading futures on that move. Important to note that into lunch and beyond sudden reversals from nowhere more likely, so hard stops needed on futures and not options friendly. 

You don’t need a strong reason to stay out, gut feeling is enough in addition to technicals, but you sure as shit need a strong reason to enter.

Old me got a reward from taking a trade as well as winning, new me is trying to stay neutral and get a reward from following rules. Trader vs gambler.

SORRY THESE POSTS ARE SO LONG... I'm not doing great right now, I will try to shorten these as I work through the challenges.

author: Porter
content:
I should have mentioned this earlier, but I did **not** intend to use the 5-minute timeframe for the entry not on this trade itself.

* The trade was meant to be a **break and retest of the pre-market level (PML)**.
* The **5-minute level** was only being used as a **price target** in this situation.
* At the time, the ticker was **below both the PML and the PDL**, with **very bearish price action** from the prior day.
* Because of that, I felt using the **PML as resistance** made sense.
* Price had **wicked into the EMA and VWAP twice and rejected**, which reinforced that bias.

I should also clarify:

* I trade primarily on the **3-minute chart**, not the 1-minute.
* That said, after dropping down to the **1-minute**, I see what you mean about the entry — and you’re right.

Looking back at it now using **TradingView replay**:

* The **9:36 candle on the 3-minute** was the correct entry.
* I entered **too late**, mainly due to hesitation on my part.
* There also wasn’t any real **displacement** at the point where I entered.

Honestly, the only way this trade would’ve worked — now that I think about it — was:

* Entering on the **second retest of the wick during the formation of the 9:36 candle**.

One question for you:

* When price is **below both the PML and the PDL**, do you **prioritize the 5-minute levels**, or is that **circumstantial** for you as well?
* And you’re absolutely right about **shorting at the key level**.

Sorry — ChatGPT helped me write this 😅

---

author: Huy
content:
first candle rule (5 mins opening orb range) is something that is always good to map out and see so you can build some context. If the price is below both the PDL and PML, that would be one confluence for me. I like to take key levels into consideration such as the HTF levels. I am going to steal Jack's words but WMT offered that beautiful pop and fade today. 
PDL + 128.10 key support would have been a low risk entry. PML and PMH should be used with caution though since the levels created in this session does not have a lot of volume so its a thing we can look at if there's a HTF thesis and PM levels is the only thing that we can work off in a *trending* day.

if presented with this trade, I would only take a short off the 128.10 level that I mentioned either or go long if it can break and retest above 129.70 since this would violate the current structure and can shift for the upside. 

p.s. if you trade with wmt more in the future, you can watch the XLP index for another confluence

author: Kam
content:
I’d probably look at the 9:42 candle after the hammer at the 5m OR as the best retest. It’s aggressive (ahead of the 1m OB), but it has confluence with a prior daily level from the prior Fri high and is inline with the 1st pullback method

The 2nd and 3rd pulls look weaker to me because momentum hadn’t really established yet - GOOG was consolidating and the market was choppy. Retests tend to work best after clear displacement & quick pullbacks

IMO the 2nd best momentum retest came later around 11:20 on the 5m once relative strength was obvious.

Big thing though is replaying & practicing these moments in real-time speed. In hindsight a lot of things can look textbook, but we have to go with what we can see in the moment! Just because the 9:42 entry didn't go up & have continuation doesn't mean it was a bad trade according to the system. The 2nd & 3rd entries look good because they went up, but for every chart that looks like this, there's one that fails to the downside also

author: Huy
content:
i see what you were trying to do. Good attempt but here's the issues. You did the right thing by waiting for good price action but 9:45am est candle had a major wick which means sellers really brought it down. 
418 is a pivot point so the idea is;
- we need to see a candle pushing past the 9:45am candle
- the above will in turn go past 418
- this is still tricky since we are walking into an order block from 9:33am

author: Huy
content:
As many of you have already agreed on this - no more 0's! No ifs. The trade has to work out right away and the move has to be impulsive for 0's to work in our favor. Also it takes another level of mental capital to be able to hold through the ups and downs. Only Jack and Tony can get away with this due to where they are in their trading journey + their experience. My suggestion is only take 0's once you get to a certain point but the size is going to be much smaller. Vast majority of us including myself are not there yet.

I can give you 2 feedbacks on the trade today. 
1. NVDA was consolidating after we got in but it never broke market structure. There was an order block that it was respecting nicely
2. QQQ also never broke structure

do not get me wrong, today was also tough. It takes some mental fortitude to hold through that

I watch and trade this stock nearly every day. If I had waited for a break & retest the trade would have failed, because it reversed hard. My stop was the low of the candle I bought in on. 

It's been my observation that with the lack of continuation, trades need to be entered earlier. There of course was a nice short immediately after the stock popped, and then subsequent buying again around 10:30. 

So that was my game plan today. Hope this helps.

author: Annie Nguyen
content: Can i ask you a question: If we trade with at least 1 DTE, will our stop loss remain the same, or should we give it more room? thank you in advance !

author: Huy
content: Are you comparing how the SL should be for let’s say 1 DTE vs 4?

author: Annie Nguyen
content: Yes bc with 0dte we give it a tighter SL, but for trade 2dte or 4dte idk how we calculate our SL

author: Huy
content: The sliding scale is your SL is closer to the 10-20%  mark the further your expiration is. The closer the expiration the wider your SL % is. Some people still give 20-40% for 1 DTE. But really, focus on market sound structure and pick a later expiration if possible. This will allow you to mentally stay in the position longer.

author: Joebag009
content: Hello friends, a few of you have been so kind to engage with me and you might remember I have not been doing well both in trading and personally. I haven't posted in a while so here is an update: I have kept journalling in crazy detail the technicals, the feelings before duirng and after the trade, pages each day and often on paper so it was just too much to share on here daily. I am trying to improve my consistency, avoid losses, and follow rules - trading really is you vs you and a journey. I slowed down my losses to basically breakeven since about mid February, but still not reverted to steady profitability like I hoped. The market has been difficult these past weeks as you all know, also a host of bad luck for me: internet outages at terrible times that stop out otherwise banger trades, broker glitches, and off the screen for personal matters and funerals on almost all the continuation days. Yesterday I got stopped out by 1-5 ticks multiple times... literally 2 points into my stop and then the market did that 200+ point pump, and stopped me on an attemt to re-enter on the pullback by a couple points. I am grinding away some evals on props and fighting with my broker. They admitted an outage and it cost me about $1000 and hope I can recover more than a small credit they offered. I am done with that broker now and will come back to options after I switch. IKBR has just not been great for me, but I'll re-watch Jack and Tony's content on it. I'm also considering CMC markets. Any recommendations? Must work for Alberta, Canada and bonus if it integrates with Ninja Trader. I keep watching the streams daily to stay in touch with everything and hope to trade again soon. Without getting into detail, I worked my way through two degrees, had a 6 figure job, and now my life is in shambles. If you are doing well treasure it and sock away some cash for a rainy day, without my emergency funds I would be living in a cardboard box right now.

author: Mark A
content: Hello Joe, I know this will sound "cliche" but don't quit.  You only loose when you quit.  I pray you find your edge and thru that consistency, profitability and peace.  Blessings brother!  The building blocks are all here between the modules, lessons, videos, lives, they are all here.

author: 305 Trader
content: one of the secrets of staying alive as an Options Trader, is to lose small. So I share with you a trick from observation. When you take an entry and the stock moves in your direction but wicks out a lot, this means there is still indecision. Do not get caught in that fight. Exit at small green or small red and wait for conviction from your Buyers. You can always reenter, if you were correct the market will go the way you needed and if it doesnt you were out already.

author: 305 Trader
content: we forget often that is better to lose small and to reenter than to lose big and being in a  drawdown from which it is difficult to recover in the same day.

content:
Hello everyone, I’ve been on this forum for six or seven months now. I’ve figured out why I can identify every single trade made by Tony and Jack, and I’m able to follow their moves accordingly. Thanks you <@864287214946746368>  I think the question of whether to [wait for the candle to close] before stopping out, as well as the pacing of taking profits, is actually very critical for traders with small accounts. It’s something I’ve been thinking about a lot, and I really resonate with it.

Also, I’m trying to fully replicate Tony’s trading approach. I’m still very curious why my risk-reward ratio is so different from his. I think there might be two reasons. First, as <@1080561490576285706>  mentioned earlier, I may have been using out-of-the-money options, while he mainly trades in-the-money options. Second, I wonder whether there might be a slight delay—maybe one or two seconds—when he’s speaking during his livestream.

author: 305 Trader
content: ok out of the money, just right below At the money is best. Then 2nd is your RvR consideration. Reftain from taking a trade les than 1:3 especially when learning when to enter and when to exit. 1:3 is to your ultimate but realistic take profit zone, and, make sure your initial RvR to HOD or LOD is 1:1. This is so you have less of a gamble situation and more of a high prob trade. This is also taught by <@1325884177291280475>

author: 305 Trader
content: so from your entry to HOD or LOD, 1:1 RvR (when applicable), but always as long as your ultimate TP zone is 1:3 from your entry.try to implement this, you will start noticing the progress right away.

author: Henry

Someone told me that Hyenuk the Korean/Colombian trader who teaches in Spanish applies the rule of 8% of his contracts size for everything, either swings or intraday. author: 305 Trader
content: hi no one answered to you, and that’s not nice. I am going to try to give you my insight. Firstly, when taking a pullback or a market correction to the d/s in the indices or stock, make sure the 1H TF is in alignment with that thesis. In a very bullish trending market as it was today or this week, taking a PUT just because your 1 min TF and  the 5 min TF tell you so, can be a trap. Just examine SPY and QQQ today 04-22 and you will agree on what I write.  Price can just pull down to an area of liquidity and then boom bounce right back to continue climbing or just be trapped in a lateral movement. Easiest trades are when you take them with the trend. And if no trend is clear, just wait, do nothing. Check other symbols in your watchlist on the 5 min and 15 min TF.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok I understand <@1490749230837399662>  Ok, you see those green lines are very close to each other. It is probably better for both educational, sharpening of skills and clarity, that you can try removing this indicator from Trading View on all these PDHs and PDLs and draw your own, just like Jack teaches it in his videos in Youtube and also the Saturdays AM recaps which for some reason he has stopped doing them. But older videos are there. Combine PDHs with zones or areas, as you can see all those three green lines are one area easily of demand.

author: 305 Trader
content: Tony also taught this one Youtube Video, go find that video on drawing areas of liquidity: From PDsH and PDsL from the D chart, then in the 1 H and me 10 mins (you can use 15 mins) you can adjust those horizontal lines of PDsH and PDsL and highlight a rectangle or zone of confluence in the 1H and 15 mins TFs. You will notice this method of drawing your areas of Supply and demand are very accurate and respected by the institutional money. But if you let Trading view draw these many horizontal lines for you, you will not get sharp on this part of your market preparation.

author: 305 Trader
content: Then the creamy area for 5 ORB, is helpful, I think a 5 ORB and a 15 min ORB can be useful, though you will notice that the method I described above is reliable and for me is more of a high prob. because is based on identifying points of confluence in the past and recent past and connecting the, on rectangles around a PDsH or PDsL horizontal lines.

author: 305 Trader
content: The more confluence an area has overtime, the more powerful it is since institutions respect them because they created them basically. Whereas, a 5 min or 15 min ORB is only relevant to that day you are trading.

author: 305 Trader
content: Here, this is how I learned it from both Jack and Tony. Now choose three colors only for horizontal lines, in this case there are more because of the ATHs levels we are having, but typically I use:  Red for PDsH (Supply area is usually red), Green for PDsL (demand area is usually green). Tony uses it inverted but I think is more accurate Red = Top = Supply  and Green = Botom = Demand. Then I use a third color to identify a strong zone of multiple points of confluence over time whether on the D chart but mostly on the 1H TF found, and then I proceed to connect those horizontal lines with a relevant area of confluence on a lower TF  for me being the 10 mins TF, but you can use the 15 mins  TF. This is the most important part of your morning routine in preparation for the market opening, identifying where those areas of liquidity are there.

author: 305 Trader
content: the area of confluence of I mean = turquoise rectangle.  This is established from an intermediate TF, being the 15 mns or the 10 mins TF.

author: 305 Trader
content: Hope this helps.

author: JP400
content: Good morning. The black line is the 8 EMA based on the Chart TF (so 1min in this case). I use this in conjunction with the pink line which is the 3 EMA also based on the Chart TF (so 1 min in this case). I want the 3 to be above the 8. If I notice the 3 and 8 constantly crossing, I am hesitant. If the 3 holds above, then I feel more confident that the price will hold in the direction I intend. The orange dotted line is the VWAP, and I want price to be on my side of it. The blue solid line is the 3 EMA based on a 15min TF. In theory it reminds me that the 15m is also holding my direction (as long as I pay attention to it). But, as with anything... Sometimes my eyes completely ignore them when desperation sets in. Call it blind panic.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok, try to come up with a way to simplify your charts. Less shaded areas and dotted lines. Stay with the essential. Since you use EMAs, I think thats great. But the problem is that the 3 EMA is too fast, and the 3 EMA in the 15 min TF is too slow, and you are in between them both with an 8 EMA. 15 min 3 EMA = 45 bars avg price of 1 min in 1 min TF. I suggest, you can rid of EMA 3 in 1 min TF, rid of 3 EMA in 15 min and work with an EMA that can pair with your 8 EMA, ie, 21 EMA or 20 SMA. Then, you can see how much they help you find an entry and navigate a trade.

author: Kam
content:
I think the key is to really switch the focus to the logic or 'why' behind the level. The reason we trade BnR and key levels is because we are looking for evidence of prior resistance turning to support and vice versa. The lower wick on the 9:56 candle shows that buyers are stepping in to defend 211 - a key level both intraday as well as last week's high. There were multiple confluences here. 3c close below such a key level on a $200 underlying price wouldn't be enough evidence to stop out for me personally. But if your data & backtested rules say to stop out, then definitely stop out!  

That being said - it's really easy to use hindsight bias on this one as the trade worked. If you were in the trade from <@722212337460838421> entry and holding as that lower wick forms, likely you're going to want to stop out at lows as it pushes below your stop. The confidence to place stops consistently and execute usually comes from really understanding & internalizing the logic behind the level and of course there's no substitute for a lot of reps where you practice doing the same thing repeatedly. I'd really suggest checking out Jack's video on accepting risk - it's a really good one and worth reviewing https://discord.com/channels/1218766394997346395/1222375088318447637/1467221561898569911

It is the art of trading 🙂 It is a shame that I am more of an engineer than an artist ! I suspect I would find this easier. author: Martin_AT
content:
One day later I looked at the trade again and I found out the mistake I made and why it confused me. Simply the entry was too late. I make this mistake from time to time and this usually leads to a bad RWR. So when something like yesterday happens my SL gets triggered and then price moves to TP.
So the logical "solution" for me is to really focus on entries very close to the level and if the trade is already gone not tho chase the trade anymore.
In the screenshot you can see that if you just move the Long-Tool a bit lower then the trade would have worked out. So simple in hindsight but sometimes very hard to understand during the "hectic" trading session. Therefore will adapt my "daily remainder" so that I can avoid this in the future.
Maybe you had the same issue and this helps you as well

content:
for me the entry point was was also good BUT only the timing was off and this can be a huge downside. So here is the entry that would have been optimal for me. It's the exact same SL/TP only with a slightly earlier entry. During the same time QQQ was also moving up after creating a potential HL in the 1min.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/BURYd40B/

Earlier entry also means that the SL is lower. In this case the SL would have been just below the 5min range (so a reasonable SL compared to the first screenshot). No candle close below the SL and therefore this entry would have worked.
Such a "small" detail but in this case this led to a loosing day for me instead of a winning day. In options trading sometimes just a few seconds can make a huge difference. This is just another example for me that I need to be fast on my entries and avoid entries that are too late / too far away from the level. If however a late enry can not be avoided then my remainder is to simply use a wider stop which would have also worked in the nvidia set up. The learning/refining of ones playbook never stops xD

author: 305 Trader
content: Good morning. You were correct, the bottom was near but not quite at the time of your entry. It was at your exit.  Go in higher Time Frames, 4H and 1H with extended hours and find zones of solid confluence and draw them as rectangles as Jack does. Polish them in 15 mins TF around their edges. They will indicate bottoms and tops for larger moves. You need only few, maybe three max, 4. When the stock breaks those levels with a retest and making new higher lows (long) or new Lower highs (short) prior your entry, plus increasing volume and a solid reaction ( a hammer or a solid large bar),its a confirmation the reversal  or continuation is in place. This is how big moves are caught. If you only wait for this and do nothing, absolutely nothing else in your trading, you will transform your account. Then, study, study off time how the best break outs happen out of those levels and refine your entry. Go back in time in your Time frame of preference and one higher to study these breakouts from these zones you drew your zones exactly as I mentioned and study how the breakout took shape and what is the perfect set up for your entry. Last one, take this model and specialize in only one stock. Once you have dominated this (with $$$ to demonstrate it), you can add more stocks to your watch list. Of course, we often do what we want and think is correct, but if you would just listen to this advice, I promise you, you can get rich. This is exactly what I am doing now.

author: 305 Trader
content: yes, study this. And by the way, depending on the type of bar and its size breaking out and volume, from those key levels, for a large move, there may not be retest, so you will need to judge on your entry. This is the art of trading but based on backtesting, fancy word for a manual inspection of past moves. I rode the QQQ brrakout for instance on market break today without needing the retest, this is one example. Just study the above.  Its all based on HTF and shape and size of the break out bar(s).

author: Humantorch
content: If you do marker order, I've been there myself many times. It's a mental game as much as it is technical. Focus is a big part of it. Once you see price starts moving to your TP, you have to get ready with the finger on a trigger. Sometimes the move happens slowly and you have lots of time to close it. Other times there are pops in price and you have to be fast to close your position otherwise it MAY reverse completely and you don't get a chance to close it, wait for maybe another chance and it goes red and you end up losing the trade.

author: Humantorch
content: I think a big part of it is realizing your mistakes and weaknesses, and trying to improve them. Improving execution and mental focus game is one of the most important parts after you learn the technicals. And nobody is perfect enough to be on point every time, so we keep improving and refining and coming to the battlefield ready and focused.

author: Dice56
content: I’m now 2 weeks into this group and so far one of my issues is that when I go into a trade it takes me a moment to see the price of the contract and to fill it and I’m wondering if I’m better off just going with a market order for the sake of speed and getting into the trade faster and out faster too when it’s time to scale.

author: Rodo
content: Honestly at this stage, just use market orders. You’re still learning, so speed and execution matter more than getting the perfect fill. Right now the bigger issue is hesitation and missing entries—not a few cents on price.Once you get comfortable with that and everything starts to feel more natural, then you can build on it step by step—and eventually switch over to limit orders as you refine your execution. Also, it depends on your size. If you’re trading a small number of contracts, market orders won’t hurt you much. But as you start trading more contracts, getting filled efficiently matters a lot more—so that’s when limit orders become more important.

author: 305 Trader
content:
ok. Two things. One is from pure observation and keeping stats and the 2nd one is a recent development. 

-  when the adverse force prior your entry, is a dwindled and reduced candle, you can safely enter on your rising bar color which takes out that adverse coor bar. So then, to that extent you entered late. But, since the previous bars were evidently with long wicks it means, market indecision. So if you take an entry following those wicks, you have to off load heavy weight at the HOD or top of those wicks as an options trader. If futures, not as critical. 

- You have to have a thesis of the PM iin 1 HR tf if you are to enter in the firsr 30 mins of the market open, what was the structure formed? The price after the opening,  will revisit those PM levels and react strongly. Check on this in addition to the PD key levels.

author: 305 Trader
content: also, it does look you grabbed that move up doing its last impulse. HTF of PD and PM need to be studied every morning prior Opening to avoid being one of  the last of the mohicans

author: Jatin
content:
You have already answered your questions. The contracts on NVDA move a lot less if you compare to AMD contracts. Just an example, if you are trading 4 cons in AMD, the usual risk is around $60 per contract. Say you lost that trade and on 4 contracts it will be -$240 on that trade.

Then you trade 4 contracts on NVDA, with usual risk being $30 per contract. Say you won this one and took a full exit at 2RR. Now you won and profit comes to $240 for your 4 contracts.

See now you are break even even after you took 2RR winner. The issue is sizing. On the first trade you were risking $240 but on second one you were risking $120.
To match the risk in the trades, you would have traded 8 contracts on NVDA with risking same $240 on the trade, now if you won at 2RR, you would have profitted $480

Let me know if this makes sense

You don’t risk the full position but mostly about 20% 
To keep it simple I use easy numbers. 
So when one contract is like 250$ and you buy 4. position size is like 1000$ but you won’t risk that. But mostly about 20% so 250$ is the risk. When you aim for 1:2rr your TP is at 500$ that’s about 50% on your whole position. 

But you have to find a way to calculate this for you. There are multiple ways of doing it. Since I’m trading 100% on tradingview I can easily calculate this directly on the chart. I don’t know how you trade but you need to find a way to quickly calculate this

author: Jatin
content:
<@1455167515926728907> <@1492137844276334827> What I have been following is very simple to follow. Helpful for small accounts, this will lead to more consistency than home run trades. Try to follow this:
If you have 1-3 cons, take full exit at 2RR. If you have 4 or more, scale out minimum 75% of position on 2RR and leave rest for next targets. This will make it simple to follow, focuses on consistent base hits instead of rare mooshots we aim for sometimes with greed or whatever.

The number of cons depend on the risk amount on cons for the trade

author: Kam
content:
Yeah that’s one of the harder parts with options. The cons don't always move with the underlying especially on fast moves, wide spreads, or later in the week. I have rules to size down on Thu, Fri even tho cons look cheaper

Another idea is to think  in “risk bands”, e.g' if your intention is to risk $100, accept that your real risk is likely $100-$175 depending on the contract dynamics

It’s never perfectly precise with options unfortunately, part of the business. We definitely all lose more than intended on those kill candles when they happen though. But with the right tactics you can put up some safeguards

author: 305 Trader
content: The higher prob set up for an entry, is not just any type of B&R, but rather, the B&R and continuation of the top of a price structure formed (ceiling), or the inverse, the B&R and continuation  of the bottom of the structure formed (floor). When you wait for this to happen you will win most of the time. Thus, the best entry in your screenshot was later when the $400 level was broken up, retested and continuation. Do this, over and over, repeat.

author: 305 Trader
content: This is what the market does. We just wait for this.

author: 305 Trader
content: it is not because it was $400 the level. It can be any price level,  just allow  the structure to formed first as it happened in your move. Read what I wrote several times. Then backtest this event in the TF you like. Log your results. It happens in both directions. This is the highest prob set up.

author: nasman
content:
Hey everyone, wanted to share a transparent update. I’ve officially wiped the $1k I started with.

This wasn't a failure of the strategy or the course, it was 100% my execution. My journal shows a "death by a thousand cuts" from:

- FOMO & Chasing: Entering late and forcing setups.
- IBKR slips / accidental execution
- Revenge Trading: Trying to "claw back" small losses and emotionally reacting.
- Stop Loss Management: I honored my stops, but didn't adjust/edit them properly for the price action.

Fortunately I only traded one contract and kept my position size small at all times. 

I’m stepping back to reset and build back the right way while trying to refine my edge. 

For the mentors and experienced traders:
1.    How do you mentally reset after a wipeout to avoid a "deficit mindset" on the next trade? I do see myself holding biases from losses. 

2.    What’s your best drill for rebuilding entry discipline?

Appreciate any feedback. Back to work!

I had a white live as a pivot point from Friday that it broke below retested on the two-minute chart along with it dropping below the VWAP and 9 EMA.  My reluctance we because at the time QQQ was moving upward.  I just rewatched the bootcamp about relative strength weakness.  Was this a decent thesis or was I lucky and this isn’t a decent setup?  When do we break away from the relative S/W of QQQ for a trade like this?  I didn't take the trade

author: Kam
content:
Hey there, 

The fact that you can clearly identify the actual behaviors so specifically is probably more important long term than the $1k itself.

For me personally, the biggest reset after rough patches has always been reducing the pressure to “make it back” and just getting laser focused on execution - especially if I know execution was the issue (vs. market or normal variance). I usually size down until I see that I'm trading my process / setups consistently again. 

Regarding drills - I think this depends on each person to a certain extent, but something that helped reduce my trade count was really focusing on my filters for (dis)qualifying a setup. If you try and actively ask yourself 'why not' when considering a trade it can help with awareness & logic vs just getting locked in on the patterns we've been trained to recognize in the charts. 

Hope that helps - love the attitude 💪

author: Kam
content:
Looks like a solid technical setup to me. Overall I'd say the HTF's on AMZN are more neutral so if you can find any pockets of space to play level to level like you did yesterday can def find some decent trade ideas. 

Generally speaking you want to be playing on the same side as the indices when trading the mega-caps. If you knew the catalyst for the downside and had conviction it would help, but otherwise I'd say lower probability if playing against the index

author: nasman
content:
That "why not" filter makes perfect sense. I definitely get locked into just seeing the pattern and ignoring the messy context around it. I'm going to implement that exact defensive mindset to help disqualify the sloppy setups.

To build on that and really strengthen my ability to identify high probability trades, what does your personal in the moment checklist look like? Is there a specific sequence of things you check off before you finally decide to pull the trigger?

I’ve been trying to discipline myself to look for multiple confluences, like checking key levels (PDH/PDL or a 5ORB) along with VWAP, a 9 EMA crossover, and indices support (SPY/QQQ) to make sure the market is behind me. But sometimes it feels like a lot to process simultaneously when the market opens.

author: Kam
content:
Awesome, I'm glad that helped! I would honestly try to keep things as simple as possible - when you backtest or review your trades, try and really break down the logic of the trade. What makes your best trades 'good', and vice versa for your worst trades. 

For me my checklist boils down to a few categories: location, context, setup quality & trigger.

But to your point, that can be a lot to process in the heat of the decision moment, especially on a 1m chart. It takes time and a lot of practice, but I would definitely start with your data and try figure out where your strengths & weaknesses are. 

Confluences can definitely add to the probabilities, but I'd say even this depends on your specific system.

author: JP400
content:
For those that think they are the only one's who are red.... You are not.
Sadly... a red day to finish a red month... Quite the achievement when you look at SPY and QQQ for the month of May.
April was green, but May wiped it out.
November to May... overall red.

We listen... we process... we understand... we can see the mistakes... we backtest... we see what we should have done...we heed the advice of others... and then, for some reason, we throw our brains out the window and make trades that we have no business making.
And the ones that would have worked... ? We stand at the station, with a perfectly good entry, and watch the train leave without us.

If I could just figure out this blockage... What it is that causes me to do what I do...

author: 305 Trader
content: Hey JP, I dont know if you are a woman or a man, i just wanted to tell you that I felt both laugh and compassion with your message. Laugh because you said “we throw the brain out of the window”. As for the rest, sounds like you have tried many things but the one thing still eludes you is the adhering to simple rules after observing what works. The only answer is: keep trying to figure it out for your self. But, with only 1x con. No more. Forget about the notion of getting rich or making a living out of this. Just sit down and trade with just 1x con, adjust your entries. The market has turned difficult for the day trader (not the swing trader with capital to hold). Corrections are very brief, ranges are long for some symbols. so go long for the most part. I also had a bad probably worst month of the year in May. Also, because I departed from what made me profitable in 2026 to try a new strategy which proved it is not working in this market with the Elliott waves I was trying. So, what we have to do is to adhere to a set of 3-4 rules and pick up our brains from the garbage can and force it to sit, “just to sit”and to wait for the rules to present themselves in the form of a set up and hit it. But also to act early as soon as they are spotted, this is the flow, to be able to cruise in low speed for a while but to switch gear quickly when the rules present themselves or viceversa. There it lies the fluidity of the same day trader.

author: 305 Trader
content: the character of the day trader is = acuity. This takes training, trial and error to develop it. So, the only remedy is to keep going. But safely (pretty small size), until you get it.

author: 305 Trader
content: I figured out in my own understanding something that I wanted to share, perhaps it sheds a light too: Why is a retest to a break out or to a breakdown so important? Well, since a price structure is broken when a ceiling becomes a floor (call), or a floor becomes a ceiling (put), how do we know if this event (break of structure) will hold? Answer: With the retest and continuation. The retest allows you to "test" that key level becomes a wall to the opposite force, and not a revolving door that would invalidates your thesis.

author: Martin_AT
content:
Hello,
on Friday my overall bias was correct because my bias was to the downside. QQQ dropped after market open and was below PDL but I still hesitated to go short on AMD. I missed the first two order block retest opportunities. On the third order block retest I already had a bad feeling but I still went short. The trade was a looser but after 11:10 am price continued to go down.
https://www.tradingview.com/x/9jDs9Idd/
For me the learning is this (more like a "re-learning" because I should have known this): If Qs is dropping for about 30min without a significant pullback then the chance for a bigger pullback or a reversal is too high and therefore shorting stocks on the 1min TF is too high risk. Thus in future I would focus on shorting opportunities at the beginning of the session and avoid late short opportunities like AMD von Friday. (Based on all the trade reviews from Tony/Jack that I have watched I noticed that they never go short too late - like me in this case)

What are your thoughts on this? Do guys also avoid late entries or is there something else I missed? Thanks

author: 305 Trader
content: you are missing a key element, in your analysis. Its the HTF. In the 1H TF and 4H Tf identify strong levels of support and resistance (often are pivot points), and plan your trades on the reaction to those levels, as well as use them to identify your exits. That move most certainly in AMD hit an important support level at 486 in 1H Tf. Take a look and you may see this. Do not trade if you dont make a thesis based on laying out a plan on these two HTF.

author: Martin_AT
content: Thank you for reminding me of the importance of zones. Because of the recent ATH plays I neglected drawing zones because often there simply was nothing on the left side. Now where markets are pulling back these levels are getting touched again of couse. Lesson learned. Thx 😁

author: 305 Trader
content: order blocks identified in 1H and 4H are called pivot points and they are the fathers of all the stock moves. Check them out.

author: 305 Trader
content: Ok <@1490749230837399662> thanks for sharing. This is what I think it happened against you. - You did not allow a solid retest to the PDH to occur. This is a must at a very important key level such as PDH. If by waiting so the move takes off w/o you so be it. 2nd, Even if you waited for a solid retest to PDH as it happened few mins later in your chart, the PMH which was still not revisited by then, was quite close to your entry. So that dwindled further your shot at target of 1:2, for at best that entry was a 1:2 with zero error for the buyers. Wait up for larger space in your potential run, it will give you higher probs and also do not rush the retest, it has to be noticeable as per Hayden's explanations.

author: 305 Trader
content: <@1490749230837399662> Think of a retest as a "micro-accumulation", or liquidity-grabbing, so since this is what it is, the more identifiable that event is, the more significant the reaction can be of the force you need it or expect it to react shall be.

author: 305 Trader

author: 305 Trader
content: Hi JP. You trade the Qs, same as I do. Copy these levels. Study them in 4H and 1H tf and figure out why they are relevant? What did the market do in each of these levels? This repeats over and over every week.  I suggest you may delete everything else you have on that chart. Then on Monday around 9 am, find the PM levels starting from 16:00 hrs thru 09:00 hrs, these PM levels shall be the confluence of as many ceilings and floors connected by wicks and or body of the candles. This is important to complete your morning puzzle. Then you are done. Next, wait for a proper retest the way Hayden teaches it before you take an entry off of these levels during the session. You may not need anything else.

author: JP400
content:
Took QQQ  today. 716 Call (0DTE by mistake) at 9:47. Stopped out at my stop.

Took QQQ again. 717 Call (2 DTE) 9:51 open (responding to hammer of 9:50). Sold HoD. Small gain. Did not recover my loss.

Took NVDA July 2 192.5 Put at 10:00:48, but it turned in the last 12 seconds and broke back above PML. Stopped out.

NVDA tried again. Took same Con. Got nervous at 10:08/10:09 and sold for tiny gain. Of course, had I held, the trade would have worked.

NVDA broke below PDL, and put in a retest. Bought 192.5 Put again at 10:23:11. Turned and stopped me out above PDL.

I saw NVDA going at 10:04, but didn't trust myself. Especially after overall loss on QQQ. Hard to mentally isolate each trade.
And I missed what looked like a QQQ at 10:06.
But... sold at my Stops. So that feels better.

author: JP400
content:
Took NVDA.... stopped out.
Took QQQ... stopped out.

I seem to be forgetting the advice to not trade until after first 30 minutes. That would have saved me from the losses, and captured the win.
